1. 🎮 Core Gamification Philosophy (PureTask-Specific)
We are not gamifying “fun.”
We are gamifying trust, speed, quality, and reliability.

Every achievement must reinforce one of these behaviors:

Behavior	Why It Matters
Show up on time	Client trust
Communicate fast	Fewer cancellations
Finish jobs properly	Platform reputation
Earn high ratings	Higher conversion
Stay consistent	Supply stability
Avoid disputes	Cost reduction
Gamification is a behavior-control system, not a cosmetic layer.

2. 🧭 Level System (Level 1 → Level 10)
Levels represent career progression, not just XP.

XP Sources (examples)
Completed job: +50 XP

5⭐ rating: +25 XP

On-time check-in: +10 XP

Before/after photos approved: +10 XP

Streak bonuses (see later): +50–300 XP

🔰 Level Breakdown
Level 1 – New Cleaner
Goal: First success, reduce fear

Requirements:

Complete 1 job

Upload before/after photos

Rewards:

Bronze badge

Visibility unlocked

“New Cleaner Boost” (temporary ranking bump)

Level 2 – Getting Started
Requirements:

3 completed jobs

Avg rating ≥ 4.5

Rewards:

+2% payout bonus (temporary, 7 days)

Access to Early Morning jobs

Level 3 – Consistent
Requirements:

10 completed jobs

No cancellations in last 7 days

Rewards:

Permanent +1 credit/hr pricing power

“Reliable” badge

Level 4 – Trusted
Requirements:

Avg rating ≥ 4.7

20 completed jobs

Rewards:

Priority listing in searches

Reduced buffer holds (client escrow)

Level 5 – Pro Cleaner
Requirements:

50 completed jobs

≥90% on-time rate

Rewards:

Gold tier eligibility

+5% payout rate

Access to recurring clients

Level 6 – Elite Operator
Requirements:

100 completed jobs

<1% dispute rate

Rewards:

Instant payout eligibility

Featured cleaner rotation

Level 7 – Community Favorite
Requirements:

25 five-star reviews

Repeat clients ≥ 5

Rewards:

Loyalty bonus credits

Profile highlight badge

Level 8 – Master Cleaner
Requirements:

250 jobs

Avg rating ≥ 4.85

Rewards:

Platinum pricing range unlocked

Reduced platform fee

Level 9 – Top 5%
Requirements:

Percentile-based (dynamic)

Reliability score ≥ 95

Rewards:

Early job access

Highest pricing band

Exclusive badges

Level 10 – Legend
Requirements:

500+ jobs

12-month spotless history

Rewards:

Max payout percentage

Platform ambassador status

Referral revenue bonus

4. ⭐ Reviews & Ratings as a Grading Engine
Ratings should not be cosmetic.

Weighted Rating Formula (example)
Final Score =
(Avg Rating × 0.5)
+ (On-Time % × 0.2)
+ (Photo Approval % × 0.15)
+ (Dispute Avoidance × 0.15)
Rating Effects
Rating	Effect
<4.3	Visibility reduced
4.5+	Normal
4.8+	Priority ranking
4.9+	Bonus XP & pricing
Important:
Use rolling averages, not lifetime ratings, so cleaners can recover.

5. 🎁 Incentive Structure (What Actually Motivates)
Badges alone do nothing. Rewards must impact money, visibility, or effort.

High-Impact Rewards
Higher credit/hour caps

Reduced platform fee

Instant payout access

Early job notifications

Profile boost

Reduced escrow buffer

Recurring-client priority

Low-Impact (Cosmetic Only)
Profile frames

Badge icons

Titles

Cosmetics are secondary, not primary.

6. 🔁 Behavioral Loops (This Is the Magic)
Loop 1 – Earnings Loop

Do good work → Earn XP & credits → Level up → Charge more → Earn more
Loop 2 – Visibility Loop

High reliability → Higher ranking → More jobs → More reviews → Even higher ranking
Loop 3 – Streak Protection

Streak active → Fear of losing progress → Better behavior
This is why cleaners stay.

🔥 Suggestions You Should Add
Progress Bars Everywhere

“Next Reward” Teaser

Missed job pauses streak (doesn’t reset fully)

Redemption Pressure

“You’re 1 job away from instant payout access”

You’re not building a cleaning app.

Action	XP
Late arrival	−25
Cancellation <12h	−75
No-show	−250
Client dispute	−150
XP cannot be purchased.
XP decays slowly if inactive (anti-abandonment).

Reliability Score =

Excellent questions — these are exactly the right pressure points to resolve before we lock anything in. What you’re asking for now is the governance layer of the system: how leveling works, how difficulty scales, and what rewards actually do in the marketplace.

I’m going to answer this in a very structured, no-hand-waving way, and then propose a clean model you can approve or tweak.

PART 1 — HOW LEVELING WORKS (CLEAR, SIMPLE, DEFENSIBLE)
First, we need to answer this explicitly:

What does it take to level up?

❌ What we are NOT doing
❌ Not “complete every goal”

❌ Not XP math

❌ Not opaque formulas

Those systems confuse cleaners and are hard to explain legally and operationally.

✅ PROPOSED LEVEL-UP MODEL (RECOMMENDED)
Each level has 3 types of goals:
Core Goals (required)

Stretch Goal (harder, optional-but-expected)

Maintenance Rule (ongoing behavior)

🔑 To level up, a cleaner must:
Complete 100% of Core Goals

Complete at least 1 Stretch Goal

Be in good standing (maintenance rule satisfied)

This gives you:

Predictability

Difficulty scaling

A way to separate “bare minimum” from “great”

🔍 Why this works
Core goals = table stakes

Stretch goal = ambition

Maintenance = quality control

This also lets you say:

“Not everyone reaches Level 10 — and that’s intentional.”

PART 2 — WHAT “HARDER GOALS” MEAN (BY DESIGN)
You specifically said:

“We need at least 1 goal per level to be a little more difficult to achieve”

Here’s how we define difficulty, cleanly:

Difficulty levers (we will use all of these)
Volume (more jobs)

Time (longer streaks)

Quality (ratings, disputes)

Consistency (no mistakes allowed)

Rarity (percentile-based)

Each level will deliberately use a different lever so it doesn’t feel repetitive.

PART 3 — DEFINING WHAT REWARDS ACTUALLY MEAN
This is critical. Every reward must be operationally explicit.

Below are exact definitions you can hand to engineering.

🔎 REWARD DEFINITIONS (NO AMBIGUITY)
1. Priority Visibility
What it means (exactly):

Cleaner appears above non-priority cleaners in search results

Ties are broken by:

Level

Reliability score

Distance

What it does NOT mean:

Does NOT guarantee jobs

Does NOT override client choice

How it’s implemented:

Search ranking weight multiplier (e.g. ×1.2)

2. Early Job Access
What it means:

Cleaner sees new job postings 5–15 minutes earlier than others

Why it matters:

Best jobs get claimed first

Costs you nothing

3. Featured Cleaner
What it means:

Cleaner is shown in:

“Recommended Cleaners”

Rotating featured slots

Limits:

Time-bound (e.g. 48 hours)

Rotational to prevent monopolies

4. Pricing Power Increase
What it means:

Higher allowed credit/hour range

Higher add-on caps

Important:

Cleaner still chooses their rate

Platform only unlocks the ceiling

5. Reduced Platform Fee
What it means:

Platform takes a smaller % of earned credits

Example:

Standard: 20%

Reduced: 17%

Elite: 15%

This is one of the strongest long-term incentives.

6. Instant Payout Access
What it means:

Cleaner can cash out instantly

Fee may be reduced or waived at higher levels

7. Reduced Escrow Buffer
What it means:

Clients hold fewer extra credits for this cleaner

Cleaner is trusted to run longer jobs

8. Recurring Client Priority
What it means:

Client rebooking flow defaults to this cleaner

Cleaner can auto-accept repeats

9. Cash Bonuses
What it means:

One-time, milestone-based

Paid as credit bonus or payout bonus

Used sparingly, tied to:

Firsts

Major volume milestones

Long-term loyalty

PART 4 — LEVEL STRUCTURE TEMPLATE (ABSTRACT, CLEAN)
Before we fill in exact numbers, here is the template every level will follow:

LEVEL X STRUCTURE
Core Goals (must complete all)
Goal A (volume)

Goal B (reliability)

Goal C (quality)

Stretch Goal (choose ≥1)
Harder version of reliability OR

Longer streak OR

Higher rating threshold

Maintenance Rule
No suspensions

No unresolved disputes

Rolling rating above minimum

Level-Up Rewards
1 earning-related reward

1 visibility-related reward

(Optional) 1 cash or convenience reward

PART 5 — ANSWERS TO YOUR QUESTIONS (DIRECT)
❓ Is leveling up just completing 100% of goals?
➡ No.
It is:

100% Core Goals

at least 1 Stretch Goal

maintenance compliance

❓ Will every level have something hard?
➡ Yes, by design.
Every level includes at least one goal that:

requires consistency

cannot be rushed

filters out low-quality cleaners

❓ Will rewards be vague?
➡ No.
Every reward will have:

ranking logic

time scope

limits

exact effect

Full Level 1–10 table

PURETASK GAMIFICATION SYSTEM
(Locked Model — Minimal Level-Up Reward)
CORE PRINCIPLE (NOW OFFICIAL)
Level-up reward = access

Goals = money, visibility, leverage

No “confetti bonuses” just for leveling

Higher levels unlock harder goals → bigger rewards

This avoids entitlement psychology and keeps cleaners focused on actions, not status.

HOW LEVELING WORKS (FINAL)
To level up, a cleaner must:
Complete 100% of Core Goals

Complete at least 1 Stretch Goal

Meet the Maintenance Rule

What leveling up gives:
❌ No cash

❌ No instant perks

✅ Access to new goal sets with larger rewards

✅ Eligibility for higher-impact incentives

This keeps motivation forward-looking.

LEVEL STRUCTURE (FINAL, COMPLETE)

WHAT “PRIORITY VISIBILITY” MEANS (FINAL, OPERATIONAL)
Priority Visibility = ranking weight boost
Exact behavior:

Cleaner appears above non-priority cleaners

Applied only when availability & distance are similar

Ranking logic (example):

Score = Distance × Reliability × Level Modifier
Priority modifier:

Normal: ×1.0

Priority: ×1.2

Featured: ×1.4

This is:

Powerful

Fair

Not pay-to-win

WHY THIS SYSTEM IS STRONG
No abstract points

No fake rewards

No entitlement

Every benefit is earned

Every level feels meaningful

Most cleaners will plateau — and that’s good

You’ve essentially built a professional ladder, not a game.

IS THERE A BETTER WAY TO REWARD THE CLEANERS FOR REACHING THERE GOALS. PLEASE GIVE ME 3 MORE EXAMPLES OF THE SYTEM OR STRUCTURE 

Yes — there are better (and in some cases stronger) ways to reward cleaners than direct cash + static perks. Cash works, but it has diminishing motivational returns and hits margins. The best marketplaces mix reward structures so cleaners feel progress, control, and upside — not just bonuses.

Below are 3 alternative reward SYSTEMS / STRUCTURES you can use instead of or alongside your current model. Each one is proven in real marketplaces and can plug directly into PureTask.

SYSTEM 1 — 🔓 PERMISSION-BASED REWARDS (ACCESS > CASH)
(“Earn the right to better work”)

Core Idea
Instead of paying cleaners for goals, you unlock access to:

better jobs

better clients

better schedules

better economics

This turns PureTask into a career ladder, not a gig app.

How it Works
Cleaners don’t get “$20 for X”.
They get new permissions that permanently increase earnings.

Examples
Goal Achieved	Reward (Permission)
10 on-time jobs	Access to higher-paying zip codes
25 five-star reviews	Can see jobs 15 min before others
5 repeat clients	Auto-accept recurring jobs
50 completed jobs	Eligible for premium clients
90% acceptance rate	Allowed to ignore low-value jobs without penalty
Why This Is Powerful
Feels earned, not given

No direct cash cost

Creates status separation

Cleaners self-select into quality tiers

Encourages long-term behavior, not short-term gaming

Where This Fits PureTask Best
Mid to high levels (4–10)

Priority visibility

Early job access

Recurring-client systems

💡 Uber, Airbnb, and Instacart all use permission-based ladders heavily.

SYSTEM 2 — 📈 EARNING MULTIPLIERS (MAKE THE SAME WORK PAY MORE)
(“Same job, higher return”)

Core Idea
Instead of paying bonuses, you multiply earnings for a period of time.

This is psychologically stronger than flat cash because:

It rewards continued action

It compounds

It feels skill-based

How it Works
Completing a goal activates a temporary multiplier.

Examples
Goal Achieved	Reward
10 dispute-free jobs	+10% earnings for next 7 days
20 on-time cleanings	+15% earnings on weekend jobs
30-day perfect streak	+20% earnings for next 10 jobs
50 five-star reviews	Reduced platform fee for 30 days
Why This Is Powerful
Encourages immediate continued work

Cleaner thinks: “I should book more while this is active”

Much cheaper than paying cash upfront

Scales naturally with cleaner productivity

Where This Fits PureTask Best
Streaks

Reliability goals

Time-based challenges

High-demand periods (weekends, holidays)

💡 DoorDash and Uber Boosts are exactly this.

SYSTEM 3 — 🧩 CHOICE-BASED REWARDS (CONTROL = MOTIVATION)
(“You choose what matters to you”)

Core Idea
When a cleaner completes a major goal, they choose their reward from a menu.

Choice dramatically increases perceived value.

How it Works
Instead of:

“You earned X”

You say:

“Choose ONE reward”

Example Reward Menu
After completing a major milestone (e.g. 50 jobs):

Choose ONE:

$30 cash bonus

Priority visibility for 14 days

Platform fee −5% for 30 days

Early job access for 21 days

Instant payout fee waived for 10 payouts

Why This Is Powerful
Different cleaners value different things

Feels respectful and professional

Reduces frustration (“I didn’t want that reward”)

Increases emotional ownership

Where This Fits PureTask Best
Major milestones (Levels 4, 6, 8, 10)

Long-term loyalty rewards

High-performing cleaner retention

💡 Amazon Flex and Upwork use choice-based incentives heavily.

BONUS SYSTEM — ⏳ TIME-LOCKED REWARDS (URGENCY WITHOUT PUNISHMENT)
Core Idea
Rewards expire if not used, creating urgency without penalties.

Example
“You unlocked priority visibility — activate within 48 hours”

“Multiplier expires in 7 days”

“Early access valid this week only”

This drives immediate engagement.

HOW THESE SYSTEMS FIT TOGETHER (RECOMMENDED)
The strongest PureTask setup is not one system, but a blend:

Level Range	Primary Reward System
Levels 1–2	Small cash + access
Levels 3–5	Permissions + multipliers
Levels 6–8	Choice-based rewards
Levels 9–10	Permanent permissions + revenue leverage
KEY STRATEGIC INSIGHT (IMPORTANT)
💥 The best reward is not money.
The best reward is making the cleaner’s future work easier, more predictable, and more profitable.

Cash keeps people happy.
Control + access keeps them loyal.

I LIKE THE HYBRID MODEL.  DO U THINK IT WOULD BE BETTER TO REWARD THEM WHEN THEY LEVEL UP INSTEAD OF INDIVIDUAL REWARDS FOR GOAL ACCOMPLISHMENT?

Short answer: NO — rewarding only on level-up is worse than what you’ve built.
But there is a much better hybrid than what you’re currently doing.

I’ll explain why, then give you the correct hybrid structure that high-performance marketplaces converge on.

WHY “LEVEL-UP ONLY” REWARDS FAIL
If rewards happen only when someone levels up:

❌ What breaks
Long dead zones

Higher levels take weeks/months

Motivation drops between milestones

Goal blindness

Cleaners stop caring about which behaviors matter

They just grind volume

Late-stage burnout

“I’m so far from the next level, why try today?”

Gaming behavior

People optimize for level, not quality

Uber, DoorDash, and Instacart all tested this early.
They moved away from level-only rewards for this exact reason.

WHY “GOAL-ONLY” REWARDS ALSO FAIL
Pure goal-based rewards have the opposite problem:

❌ What breaks
Too many micro-rewards feel transactional

Cleaners chase bonuses, not careers

No long-term identity (“I’m a Level X cleaner”)

✅ THE CORRECT ANSWER:
LEVELS = STATUS & ACCESS
GOALS = PAYOUTS & POWER
This is the optimal hybrid and the one that fits PureTask perfectly.

THE IDEAL HYBRID MODEL (RECOMMENDED)
1️⃣ LEVEL-UP REWARDS (RARE, MEANINGFUL, NON-CASH)
Level-ups should never pay cash.
They should unlock permanent advantages.

Examples of Level-Up Rewards
Access to higher-paying job pools

Earlier job visibility windows

Higher allowed pricing caps

Lower platform fee ceiling

Eligibility for premium/recurring clients

Reduced friction (escrow, approvals, rules)

👉 Levels define your career ceiling

2️⃣ GOAL COMPLETION REWARDS (FREQUENT, TACTICAL)
Goals should deliver:

cash

multipliers

temporary boosts

choice-based rewards

Examples
“20 on-time jobs → priority visibility for 14 days”

“25 five-star reviews → $25 OR fee reduction (choose one)”

“30-day streak → +15% earnings next 10 jobs”

👉 Goals drive day-to-day behavior

HOW THIS FEELS TO A CLEANER (IMPORTANT)
Instead of:

“I’m grinding for Level 6”

They feel:

“If I keep performing well, I make more money now, and my ceiling keeps rising.”

That’s the psychological sweet spot.

THE CLEAN STRUCTURE YOU SHOULD USE
🔹 LEVELS (Permission System)
Slow

Permanent

No cash

Hard to earn

Unlock better goals

🔹 GOALS (Reward System)
Frequent

Tangible

Clear

Behavioral

Earn money, boosts, or leverage

Goals = what you actually earn

Only at Levels 4, 6, 8, 10

CORE FAIRNESS PRINCIPLES (LOCK THESE FIRST)
Every reward must obey these rules:

No reward guarantees jobs

Client choice is never overridden

Distance, availability, and schedule still matter

Advantages are relative, not absolute

All boosts are capped and/or time-bound

Performance always beats perks

This prevents “pay-to-win” or favoritism accusations.

1️⃣ PRIORITY VISIBILITY
(Most important reward — must be precise)

What it means in practice
Priority visibility affects ranking order, not eligibility.

When a client searches, cleaners are sorted using a ranking score, not a fixed list.

Example Ranking Formula
Search Score =
Distance Weight
× Availability Match
× Reliability Score
× Visibility Multiplier
Visibility Multiplier Values
Cleaner Type	Multiplier
Standard	1.00
Priority	1.15–1.25
Featured	1.30–1.40
Priority visibility means:

You appear higher than similar cleaners

You do NOT jump above:

much closer cleaners

cleaners who better match time/location

What it does NOT do
❌ Does not guarantee bookings

❌ Does not override client selection

❌ Does not hide other cleaners

Fairness controls
Only applies when distance & availability are comparable

Automatically disabled if maintenance rules fail

Time-limited unless re-earned

2️⃣ EARLY JOB ACCESS
What it means in practice
When a new job is created:

System creates job

Job enters a short pre-release window

Eligible cleaners see it first

Timing Windows
Level	Early Access
L2–3	5 minutes
L4–6	10–15 minutes
L7+	20 minutes
After the window:

Job becomes visible to everyone

Fairness controls
No one sees jobs exclusively

Clients are unaware of the timing difference

Window lengths are capped

3️⃣ FEATURED CLEANER PLACEMENT
What it means in practice
Featured cleaners appear in:

“Recommended Cleaners” carousel

Highlighted cards in search results

Rules
Rotational (no permanent monopolies)

Time-bound (24–72 hours)

Limited slots per region

Fairness controls
Rotation algorithm ensures exposure

Cleaner must remain eligible

Feature is removed if performance dips

4️⃣ HIGHER PRICING POWER
What it means in practice
The platform controls price ceilings, not prices.

Example
Level	Max Credits / Hour
L1–2	30
L3–4	35
L5–6	40
L7–8	45
L9–10	50
Cleaners:

Choose their own rate

Cannot exceed their level’s cap

Fairness controls
Clients see prices transparently

Higher price ≠ higher ranking

Market competition still applies

5️⃣ REDUCED PLATFORM FEES
What it means in practice
The platform takes a smaller percentage temporarily or permanently, depending on level.

Example
Cleaner	Platform Fee
Standard	20%
Temporary Reward	15–18%
Elite Floor	12–14%
Fairness controls
Fee floors prevent race-to-zero

Reductions tied to behavior

Automatically revoked if maintenance fails

6️⃣ INSTANT PAYOUT ACCESS & FEE REDUCTIONS
What it means in practice
Cleaners can cash out immediately after job completion

Fee reductions apply per transaction

Example
Level	Instant Payout
L6	Enabled
L8	Fee reduced
L10	Fee waived (cap applies)
Fairness controls
KYC & fraud checks mandatory

Caps on daily instant payouts

Abuse monitoring enabled

7️⃣ REDUCED ESCROW BUFFER
What it means in practice
Normally:

Client prepays extra credits to cover overruns

Trusted cleaners:

Require fewer buffer credits

Example
Cleaner	Escrow Buffer
New	+30%
Trusted	+15%
Elite	+5–10%
Why this matters
Clients prefer these cleaners

Jobs convert faster

Cleaner feels “trusted”

Fairness controls
Automatically disabled if disputes rise

Client approval still required

8️⃣ RECURRING CLIENT PRIORITY
What it means in practice
When a client rebooks:

The system defaults to the same cleaner

Cleaner can auto-accept

Fairness controls
Client can override

Cleaner can decline

Auto-accept can be paused

9️⃣ HIGH-VALUE / PREMIUM JOB ACCESS
What it means in practice
Certain jobs are flagged as:

Large homes

Deep cleans

Move-in/out

High-credit value

Only eligible cleaners:

See these jobs early

Or see them at all

Fairness controls
Eligibility based on performance

Clients still choose

No permanent exclusion

🔟 CHOICE-BASED REWARDS

Cleaner completes a major goal and chooses one:

Example menu:

$30 cash

Priority visibility (14 days)

Platform fee −5% (30 days)

Early job access (21 days)

Fairness controls
One selection only

Menu varies by level

No stacking duplicates

HOW WE ENSURE THE SYSTEM IS FAIR
Algorithmic Safeguards
Distance always matters

Availability always matters

Quality always matters

Behavioral Safeguards
Maintenance rules pause benefits

Rolling averages prevent permanent punishment

Abuse flags override perks

Transparency
Cleaner sees:

why they ranked where they did

what boosts are active

neutral, clean search results

FINAL TAKEAWAY (IMPORTANT)
You are not giving advantages.
You are rewarding reliability with efficiency.

Face less friction

Low performers:

Still get jobs

Still can improve

Are never locked out

SO HERE IS WERE I AM ALITTLE CONFUSED. WHEN WE ARE TALKING ABOUT TIMING WINDOWS. HOW DOE STHAT APPLY WHEN THE CUSTOMER IS SUPPOSED TO BE SELECTING THE CLEANER OR CLEANERS THEY WANT. ITS NOT LIKE THE CUSTOMER JUST LISTS THERE JOB REQUEST ON A BIDDING BOARD AND THEN THE CLEANERS GO FIND AND BID ON JOB 

This is a very good catch, and your intuition is right — if the customer is actively selecting a cleaner, then a naïve “job board timing window” would not make sense.

What’s happening here is that there are actually multiple matching architectures, and we need to be precise about where timing windows apply and where they do NOT.

I’m going to clear this up cleanly by:

Explaining the two different booking flows

Showing where timing windows actually fit in your model

Proposing the correct PureTask-compatible interpretation

Giving you a final recommendation so there’s no confusion going forward

1️⃣ THE CONFUSION COMES FROM MIXING TWO DIFFERENT MODELS
❌ Model A — “Open Job Board / Bidding”
Customer posts a job

Cleaners browse and bid/claim

Timing windows = who sees the job first

This is NOT what PureTask is doing.
You are correct to reject this.

✅ Model B — “Customer-Selection Marketplace” (PureTask)
Customer creates a booking

Platform presents a shortlist of cleaners

Customer selects one or a few

Cleaner confirms / accepts

This is your model.

👉 In this model, cleaners are not browsing jobs
👉 They are being surfaced to customers

That changes everything about timing.

2️⃣ SO WHAT DOES “TIMING WINDOW” MEAN IN A CUSTOMER-SELECTION FLOW?
In PureTask, timing does NOT mean “who sees the job first.”
It means:

Who gets surfaced to the customer first, and for how long.

This is a ranking + exposure concept, not a bidding concept.

3️⃣ THE CORRECT WAY TIMING WORKS IN PURETASK
Let’s walk through a realistic PureTask booking flow.

STEP 1 — CUSTOMER ENTERS JOB DETAILS
Location

Time window

Cleaning type (basic, deep, move-out)

Preferences (pets, supplies, etc.)

STEP 2 — PLATFORM GENERATES A CANDIDATE POOL
Example:

20 cleaners match location + availability

STEP 3 — TIMING WINDOW APPLIES HERE
The platform does not show all 20 equally at once.

Instead, it stages exposure:

Phase 1 — Priority Exposure Window (e.g. first 5–15 minutes)
Customer sees:

Priority / higher-level cleaners

Top reliability matches

List is still multiple cleaners (e.g. 3–6 options)

Customer can:

Select immediately

Or wait / refresh

Phase 2 — Expanded Exposure
If no selection is made:

More cleaners are added

Lower-tier or farther cleaners appear

👉 The customer is always choosing
👉 The system is just sequencing who appears first

4️⃣ WHAT “EARLY ACCESS” REALLY MEANS (REDEFINED CORRECTLY)
In PureTask terms:

Early access does NOT mean cleaners see jobs earlier
It means cleaners are shown to customers earlier

This is subtle but crucial.

Correct Terminology (Recommended)
Instead of saying:

❌ “Early job access”

Use:

✅ Early customer exposure

✅ Priority shortlist placement

Internally you can still call it “early access,” but this framing avoids confusion.

5️⃣ PRACTICAL EXAMPLES (VERY CONCRETE)
Example 1 — Standard Cleaner
Appears:

In the full list

After the first exposure phase

Still selectable

Still visible

Example 2 — Priority Cleaner
Appears:

In the first shortlist

With fewer competitors on screen

Higher chance of being clicked

No guarantee of booking

Example 3 — Featured Cleaner
Appears:

First page

Highlighted card

“Recommended” section

Still must be chosen by client

6️⃣ HOW THIS STAYS FAIR TO CUSTOMERS
Customers are NOT:
Forced to choose anyone

Blocked from seeing others

Shown false scarcity

Customers ARE:
Shown the best matches first

Able to expand or scroll

Always in control

This mirrors:

Airbnb

Thumbtack

Upwork search results

7️⃣ HOW THIS STAYS FAIR TO CLEANERS
Cleaners are NOT:
Locked out of jobs

Hidden permanently

Competing against unlimited people at once

Cleaners ARE:
Rewarded with earlier exposure

Given less crowded screens

Competing on quality, not speed

8️⃣ FINAL RECOMMENDATION (LOCK THIS)
🔒 Official Definition Going Forward
Timing windows in PureTask refer to staged customer exposure, not job bidding.
Cleaners never browse jobs.
Customers always choose.
Rewards affect when and how prominently a cleaner is shown — never exclusivity.

This keeps:

your UX clean

your rewards meaningful

your system fair

your language precise

HOW DOES THIS WORK AT SCALE. IT SEEMS TO BE  LIKE IT BREAKS DOWN WITH MORE USERS AND CLEANERS

This is an excellent concern, and you’re right to question it — most marketplaces do break when they try to “reward visibility” without thinking about scale.

The good news: the model you’re building does NOT break at scale if it’s implemented the right way.
The bad news: it will break if implemented naïvely.

I’m going to walk you through this very clearly and concretely.

THE CORE FEAR YOU’RE HAVING (AND IT’S VALID)
You’re thinking:

“If we have thousands of cleaners and thousands of customers, how can we:

stage exposure,

give priority,

keep things fair,

without the system collapsing or becoming meaningless?”

That concern comes from imagining one global pool.

👉 That’s NOT how this works at scale.

THE KEY INSIGHT (THIS IS THE UNLOCK)
At scale, there is NO single marketplace.
PureTask is actually thousands of micro-markets, segmented by:

geography (radius)

time window

job type

availability

preferences

Each booking only competes inside a very small slice of the system.

This is why the model scales.

WHAT “SCALE” ACTUALLY LOOKS LIKE IN PRACTICE
Let’s use real numbers to make this concrete.

Example: Big city, high scale
10,000 total cleaners in the city

Sounds scary, right?

But a single booking filters down to:

Filter	Remaining cleaners
Within 10 miles	~400
Available at that time	~120
Matches job type	~60
Meets client preferences	~30
Active + online recently	~15
👉 The customer is choosing from ~10–15 cleaners, not 10,000.

This holds true even at massive scale.

HOW THE SYSTEM SCALES WITHOUT BREAKING
STEP 1 — HARD FILTERS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
These are NOT rewards. These are constraints.

Distance

Availability

Job type

Required skills

Compliance

If you don’t pass these, you don’t appear.
No amount of “priority” overrides this.

This alone reduces the pool by 90–95%.

STEP 2 — SOFT SORTING (THIS IS WHERE REWARDS APPLY)
Now we’re sorting a small pool, not the entire market.

Example ranking inputs:

Reliability score

Level

Active boosts (priority, featured)

Recent performance

Important:
Priority visibility is just a multiplier, not a bypass.

That means:

Cleaner A doesn’t leapfrog 50 people

Cleaner A edges out similar cleaners

At scale, that’s exactly what you want.

STEP 3 — STAGED EXPOSURE (NOT EVERYONE AT ONCE)
This is the piece that feels fragile, but isn’t.

At scale, staging does NOT mean:
hiding thousands of cleaners

artificial scarcity

bidding wars

It means:

Showing fewer options at once to reduce choice overload.

Which is actually better UX.

WHAT HAPPENS AS THE SYSTEM GETS BIGGER?
🔹 More cleaners ≠ more competition per booking
Because bookings fragment by:

time

location

job type

The competition per booking stays roughly constant.

This is why Uber works in NYC and a small town.

WHY PRIORITY VISIBILITY DOES NOT DILUTE AT SCALE
You might worry:

“If too many cleaners earn priority, doesn’t it become meaningless?”

Only if you let it.

You won’t — because:

1️⃣ Priority is local
Applies per job slice, not globally

2️⃣ Priority is temporary
Most boosts expire

3️⃣ Priority is conditional
Paused if maintenance rules fail

4️⃣ Priority is relative
It only matters among similar candidates

At scale, relative advantages matter more, not less.

WHAT WOULD ACTUALLY BREAK THE SYSTEM (IMPORTANT)
Here are the real failure modes — avoid these:

❌ Global leaderboards
❌ Permanent top slots
❌ Rewards that ignore distance/time
❌ Showing 50+ cleaners at once
❌ Letting rewards override availability

You are explicitly not doing any of these.

WHY THIS MODEL GETS STRONGER WITH SCALE
As you grow:

More data → better reliability scoring

More segmentation → smaller candidate pools

More demand → boosts feel more valuable

More supply → incentives shape behavior more precisely

In other words:

Scale makes incentives more precise, not weaker.

HOW BIG PLAYERS DO THIS (WITHOUT SAYING IT)
Airbnb, Uber, Instacart, and DoorDash all:

filter hard

sort softly

stage exposure

reward reliability

never guarantee outcomes

They don’t talk about it — but this is the engine.

FINAL REFRAME (THIS IS IMPORTANT)
Your system does not break at scale because:

You are not rewarding access to jobs.
You are rewarding efficiency within constrained matching pools.

That’s the difference between:

a fragile marketplace

a scalable one

Simulate a high-scale city with numbers

Design the exact matching + ranking pseudocode

Stress-test edge cases (oversupply, undersupply)

Show how incentives auto-adjust as supply grows

Just tell me where you want to go next.

Priority Visibility
Your profile will be shown earlier to customers who are a good match for your availability and location. This helps you get booked faster.

Reduced Platform Fees
You’ll keep more of what you earn on each job during this reward period.

Instant Payout Access
You can cash out your earnings immediately after a job finishes.

Recurring Client Priority
When a customer books again, you’ll be shown first and can auto-accept the job.

2) API-ready goal schemas (JSON)
Below is a clean schema that supports:

Core / Stretch / Maintenance

“choose ≥ 1” rules

time windows (e.g., last 14 days)

rolling windows (e.g., last 50 jobs)

counters (e.g., 200 completed)

percentages (e.g., acceptance rate 90%)

conditions like “losing disputes”

A) Data model: GoalDefinition (store in DB)
{
  "id": "lvl3_core_jobs_completed_10",
  "level": 3,
  "type": "core",
  "title": "Complete 10 cleanings",
  "description": "Finish 10 jobs on PureTask.",
  "metric": "jobs.completed.count",
  "operator": ">=",
  "target": 10,
  "window": null,
  "scope": "cleaner",
  "enabled": true,
  "reward_ids": ["rwd_cash_20_level3"]
}
B) Supported fields (recommended)
metric is an internal string key you compute from events/DB

window supports time / job count windows

Window examples:

{ "type": "days", "value": 14 }
{ "type": "last_jobs", "value": 50 }
C) RewardDefinition
{
  "id": "rwd_visibility_priority_14d",
  "kind": "visibility_boost",
  "name": "Priority visibility (14 days)",
  "params": { "multiplier": 1.2, "duration_days": 14 },
  "stacking_rule": "extend_duration",
  "enabled": true
}
D) LevelDefinition (gating rules)
{
  "level": 3,
  "name": "Reliable",
  "requirements": {
    "core_complete_all": true,
    "stretch_complete_min": 1,
    "maintenance_require_all": true
  },
  "goal_ids": [
    "lvl3_core_jobs_completed_10",
    "lvl3_core_on_time_8",
    "lvl3_core_no_noshow",
    "lvl3_stretch_5_five_star",
    "lvl3_stretch_2_clients_3_jobs_each",
    "lvl3_stretch_zero_cancel_10",
    "lvl3_maint_no_disputes_last_10",
    "lvl3_maint_no_last_min_cancel",
    "lvl3_maint_rating_rolling_45"
  ],
  "enabled": true
}
E) CleanerProgress response (what app needs)
{
  "cleaner_id": "c_123",
  "current_level": 3,
  "good_standing": true,
  "core": [
    { "goal_id": "lvl3_core_jobs_completed_10", "progress": 8, "target": 10, "complete": false }
  ],
  "stretch": [
    { "goal_id": "lvl3_stretch_5_five_star", "progress": 3, "target": 5, "complete": false }
  ],
  "maintenance": [
    { "goal_id": "lvl3_maint_no_disputes_last_10", "status": "ok" }
  ],
  "eligible_to_level_up": false,
  "active_rewards": [
    { "reward_id": "rwd_visibility_priority_14d", "ends_at": "2026-03-01T00:00:00Z" }
  ],
  "next_best_actions": [
    { "goal_id": "lvl3_core_jobs_completed_10", "reason": "closest_core_goal" }
  ]
}
3) Admin tuning controls (what knobs you need)
You want safe adjustments without redeploys. That means an admin console that edits:

A) Goal tuning
Enable/disable a goal

Adjust target thresholds (e.g., 25 → 20)

Adjust windows (14 days → 21 days)

Adjust which level contains the goal

Adjust “choose N of M” stretch rules (rare)

Safety:

Version each goal (version, effective_at)

Never retroactively change rules for already-earned levels (or do so only for future progress)

B) Reward tuning
Reward amount ($, duration, multiplier)

Stacking behavior:

extend_duration (recommended)

replace_if_higher

no_stack

Budget caps:

daily / weekly cash payout caps

per-cleaner cash cap per month

C) Ranking tuning
Visibility multiplier values by reward

Guardrails:

“priority applies only within distance band”

“priority doesn’t outrank closer matches beyond X miles”

A/B testing toggles:

1.15 vs 1.2 multipliers in a region

D) Fraud & abuse monitoring controls
Thresholds for flags:

suspicious login streaks

unusual acceptance behavior

dispute patterns

Auto-pausing rewards if flagged (not level)

E) Analytics dashboards (admin-facing)
% of cleaners at each level

median time-to-level

which goals block progression most

incremental lift from boosts (bookings/week)

dispute rate by level

4) Extra suggestions you didn’t ask for (but you should do)
1) “Next Reward Preview” everywhere
On each goal card, show:

Unlocks: Priority visibility (14 days)

This is the #1 motivator.

2) “Explain like I’m busy” mode
Add a toggle:

“Show me only my 1–2 best next actions”

This reduces overwhelm and increases completion.

3) “Earnings Impact” attribution
Show:

Your rewards helped you get +3 bookings this week.

This makes non-cash rewards feel like money.

4) Seasonal challenges (no resets)
Example:

“Weekend Warrior Month: weekend jobs count 2× toward goals”

“5⭐ Sprint: 5-star reviews unlock choice reward”

Keeps the system fresh without undermining permanence.

5) Goal anti-gaming constraints
Examples:

Login streak counts only if:

app opened + meaningful action OR

accepted/confirmed job OR

messaged client
(Not just open/close.)

A) GoalDefinition JSON set (Core + Stretch + Maintenance)
Notes on metrics (standardized)
metric is a stable key you compute from DB/events.

window is only set when your goal explicitly states a time/job window.
If your goal does not specify a window, window: null = lifetime-to-date.

filters lets you express things like “basic only,” “deep only,” “lost disputes only,” “early morning jobs,” etc.

D) Admin UI wireframe (screens + fields)
This is the minimum admin console that lets you tune incentives safely without redeploy and without breaking trust.

1) Admin Home — Incentives Overview
Widgets

Distribution: % cleaners by level

Median time-to-level (L1→L2, L2→L3…)

Active rewards running right now

Dispute rate by level

“Blocked goals” (which goals prevent leveling most)

Controls

Global pause for cash rewards (emergency)

Global cap: max cash bonus per cleaner per month

2) Levels Manager
Table

Level number, name

Requirements (Core all, Stretch min, Maintenance all)

Maintenance enabled? (L8/L9 off)

Active cleaners at this level

Last updated version

Detail view

List goals for this level (Core/Stretch/Maint)

“Preview as cleaner” button

Fields

stretch_complete_min (default 1)

Maintenance enforcement: required_all true/false

Version + effective date

3) Goals Library
Search + Filters

Level, type, metric, enabled

“Contains window”

“Uses disputes”

“Uses login”

Goal Editor Fields

ID (immutable)

Title / description

Metric key (dropdown)

Operator

Target

Window type/value

Filters (JSON editor)

Reward IDs (multi-select)

Enabled toggle

Version + effective_at date

Audit log viewer (who changed it, when, old/new)

Safety

“Changes apply only to NEW progress after effective_at”

“Recalculate previews only” button (no real effects)

4) Rewards Manager
Reward Editor Fields

Reward kind (visibility_boost, fee_discount, cash_bonus, etc.)

Params (multiplier, duration_days, uses, etc.)

Stacking rule (extend_duration, no_stack, replace_if_higher, extend_uses)

Enabled toggle

Budget category (cash / non-cash)

Cash budget caps:

per cleaner / month

per region / month

global / day

5) Ranking & Exposure Tuning
This is where fairness is enforced.

Visibility rules

Priority multiplier values

Featured multiplier values

Guardrails:

“Priority only applies within X miles”

“Do not outrank someone closer by > Y miles”

“Availability match must be true for boosts to apply”

Exposure windows

L2: 5 min, L4: 10 min, L6+: 15–20 min

Max exposure window cap per day

A/B toggles

Region-based experiments:

multiplier 1.15 vs 1.2

early exposure 10 vs 15 minutes

6) Compliance & Abuse Controls
Flags

suspicious login streak (no meaningful actions)

abnormal acceptance patterns

dispute spikes

refund correlation

Actions

Auto-pause rewards on flag (NOT levels)

Require manual review

Temporary reward lock duration

7) Audit Log & Rollback
Every change stored as versioned diff

One-click rollback to prior version

“Explain change” required field for admins

E) Extra high-impact suggestions (you should include)
1) “Meaningful login” definition (prevents gaming)
A login counts only if within 15 minutes they do one of:

accept/confirm a job

send a client message

view job request list + open job details

upload photos / mark job steps

This prevents fake streaks.

2) Reward attribution (makes non-cash feel like cash)
Show in app:

“Priority visibility helped you get +{{X}} extra booking(s) this week.”

3) Region scaling
Tune rewards per city:

oversupply → reduce visibility boosts, emphasize quality

undersupply → increase early exposure windows to keep jobs filled

BEFORE CONTINUEING , WILL U REVIEW AND ANALZYE OUR CURRENT SYSTEM , AND ANALYZE IT WEARINGF MULTIPLE DIFFERENT HATS TO MAKE SURE WE COVER EVERY IMPORTANT  ASPECT AND FUNCTION  OF PURETASK. THEN PROVIDE ME WITH A LIST OF POTENTIAL IMPROVEMENT I COULD AND SHOULD MAKE . OR ANY OTHER SUGGESTION U MIGHT HAVE FOR OTHER REASONS

Absolutely. I’m going to review your current Level 1–10 system wearing multiple hats and check it against what a scalable PureTask marketplace needs: product UX, marketplace economics, ops, fraud/risk, engineering feasibility, and cleaner psychology. Then I’ll give you a prioritized improvement list.

I’m evaluating the system you currently have (Core/Stretch/Maintenance + hybrid rewards + staged exposure concept), not inventing a new one.

1) Product Manager Hat — Does it cover the full PureTask experience?
✅ What you cover extremely well
Core workflow mastery: accept → message → clock in/out → photos → complete job

Quality loop: ratings/reviews, 5-star volume, low disputes

Marketplace behaviors that matter: on-time, no cancellations, acceptance rate

Power-user usage: repeat clients, deep cleans, move-in/out, weekend/early slots

Retention hooks: login streaks + long-term goals

⚠️ What’s under-covered (surface area gaps)
These are meaningful parts of a cleaning marketplace that aren’t strongly represented:

Profile & service quality inputs (not outcomes)

supplies readiness, equipment, checklists, “what’s included”

certifications/training modules (even lightweight)

preferred job types & boundaries (pets, stairs, heavy lifting)

Customer experience micro-actions

confirming arrival (“I’m on my way”)

mid-job updates (“Finishing kitchen now”)

post-job follow-up (“Anything else you want adjusted?”)

Availability discipline

updating availability calendar

reducing last-minute “unavailable” declines

schedule reliability beyond acceptance rate

Add-ons / upsells (platform revenue)

add-on attach rate (fridge, oven, laundry, etc.)

upsell without being spammy

Takeaway: you cover the “core loop” strongly, but could better cover service professionalism and revenue-per-job behaviors.

2) Marketplace / Economics Hat — Does this maximize revenue + minimize disputes?
✅ Strong alignment
Goals heavily incentivize: completion, on-time, low cancellations, low disputes

Rewards (visibility/exposure/fees) are mostly margin-friendly compared to pure cash

⚠️ Biggest economic risk: cash reward creep
You’ve attached quite a few cash amounts across the ladder. At scale, it can become:

expensive

hard to predict

easy to “optimize for bonuses”

Fix without removing cash: convert many cash rewards into choice rewards (cash OR boost) and cap cash monthly.

⚠️ Missing lever: “profit per booking”
Right now, progression rewards volume + quality. That’s great.
But you can improve platform economics by adding metrics like:

high-value job mix (deep/move-out)

add-on attach rate

repeat booking conversion

Takeaway: you’re revenue-aligned on volume; add 1–2 levers for revenue quality.

3) Cleaner Psychology Hat — Is it fun, motivating, and fair?
✅ Very strong motivation structure
clear ladder

identity levels (rare top levels)

stretch goals give choice

maintenance pauses (not punitive level loss)

⚠️ Potential frustration points
Login streak goals can feel artificial
If a cleaner is busy or takes a vacation, streak goals can feel punishing.
Even though levels don’t drop, motivation can.

Acceptance rate requirements can feel unfair
If PureTask sends low-quality jobs (bad distance/time), acceptance targets feel like coercion.

Rating percent goals
“95% rating” is conceptually confusing unless you standardize it.

Fix: define “good faith declines” and make streaks forgiving (more below).

Takeaway: system is motivating, but needs anti-frustration rules so it feels fair.

4) Ops / Support Hat — Will this reduce tickets and disputes in real life?
✅ Strong
photo verification goals reduce he-said/she-said

dispute goals create strong incentive to prevent issues

messaging goals reduce miscommunication

⚠️ Missing operational safety net: “dispute prevention habits”
The system rewards low disputes, but doesn’t always teach the habits that prevent them.

Add soft goals/badges like:

“Pre-clean checklist confirmation”

“Customer walkthrough complete”

“Issue flagged before completion” (prevents blow-ups)

Takeaway: add 3–5 optional goals/badges that encode best practices, not just outcomes.

5) Risk / Fraud Hat — Can people game it?
✅ Good fundamentals
outcomes-based metrics are harder to fake than points

windows like “last 50 jobs” reduce one-off gaming

⚠️ Most gameable items
Login streaks
If “login” is counted as app open, it will be gamed.

Message count
If it’s just a count, they’ll send spam (“ok”, “hi”).

Photo uploads
If not validated, could be low-effort junk.

Fixes (low effort, huge payoff)
“Meaningful login” rule (login counts only with an action)

“Meaningful message” rule (character minimum or client response)

Photo verification: require before + after AND a minimum count OR a checklist

Takeaway: you need anti-gaming definitions, not new goals.

6) Engineering Hat — Is this implementable without pain?
✅ Yes, but standardize metrics
Your schema approach is solid. The main engineering risk is metric ambiguity:

What is “on-time” (GPS radius? grace period?)

What is “lost dispute” (status enum?)

What is “rating percent” vs star average?

What is “move-in/out” job type (tag?)

Recommendation: create a single “Metrics Contract” doc that defines each metric.

Takeaway: implementability is fine as long as you lock metric definitions.

7) Brand / Trust Hat — Is it defensible and explainable?
✅ Very defensible
client choice is preserved

boosts are relative, not absolute

no demotions

behavior-based not pay-based

⚠️ Watch out for perception:
“priority visibility” can be perceived as unfair unless you explain:

“You’ll be shown earlier to customers you’re a strong match for.”

Takeaway: transparency copy matters.

PRIORITIZED IMPROVEMENTS (HIGH → LOW)
Tier 1 — Must-do before launch (high impact, low cost)
Define “meaningful login”

login day counts only if they complete an action (accept job, message, open request, upload photos, etc.)

Define “meaningful messages”

message goal counts only if:

≥ N characters, OR

customer replies, OR

message is selected from quick-actions (“On my way”, “Arrived”, “Finished”)

Add “Good Faith Declines”
So acceptance rate doesn’t feel coercive:

allow declines without penalty for reasons like:

too far

schedule conflict

unsafe location

job type mismatch

cap good-faith declines per week

Standardize ratings
Pick ONE:

stars average (e.g., 4.8)

OR percent (e.g., 95%)
Using both will confuse cleaners and complicate support.

Clarify On-time measurement

define GPS radius + grace period

define “clock in/out success” criteria

Tier 2 — Strongly recommended (improves revenue + reduces disputes)
Add 1 revenue-quality lever
Without overcomplicating:

“Complete X deep cleans” (already present)

Add add-on completion goal at mid levels:

“Complete 10 add-ons” (not upsell; completion)
This improves revenue per booking.

Dispute prevention micro-goals
Optional stretch/badges:

“Customer checklist confirmed”

“Before/after photos approved 10x” (already)

“Flagged issue before completion” (prevents dispute escalation)

Introduce “Choice Rewards” more widely
Replace many cash-only rewards with:

cash OR boost OR fee reduction
This reduces cost and increases perceived value.

Tier 3 — Nice-to-have (boosts fun + engagement)
Progress UX: “Next Best Action”
Always show 1–2 highest ROI goals.

Seasonal challenges (no resets)

weekend jobs count 2× this month

5-star sprint

Badge layer
Non-economic “pride badges” for identity and retention.

Answers to two implied strategic questions
Should maintenance rules “control a lot”?
Yes — if they only pause rewards and progress, and are transparent.
This is your safety valve to protect quality without punishment.

Should you ever reset all cleaners to 0?
No. Never.
Do seasons/challenges instead.

One big suggestion you didn’t ask for (but you should do)
Create a “Marketplace Health Governor”
An admin control that can adjust reward strength by region:

if oversupply: reduce visibility multipliers slightly, emphasize quality goals

if undersupply: increase early exposure windows and reduce strictness temporarily

This prevents the system from becoming rigid as markets change.

Metrics Contract v1
A table that defines every metric key (on-time, dispute lost, rating percent, login day, meaningful message) so engineering + support + ops are aligned.

Anti-gaming rules spec
Short definitions that prevent loopholes for login, messages, photos, acceptance.

0) System Overview: What we’re implementing

1) Tier 1 Must-Do BEFORE Launch (High impact, low cost)
1.1 “Meaningful login” definition (anti-gaming)
Rule
A “login day” / “login streak day” counts only if the cleaner performs at least one meaningful action within 15 minutes of opening the app.

Meaningful actions (counts)
View job request list AND open a specific job request detail

Accept / decline a job request (with reason)

Send a message to a client (meaningful message rules apply)

Confirm “On my way” / “Arrived” / “Job complete”

Upload before/after photos

Update availability calendar (at least 1 time block change)

Does NOT count
App open + close

Background open / push notification tap without action

Implementation
Track engagement.session_started

Track engagement.meaningful_action_performed

A streak day increments only if session has ≥1 meaningful action

Metric keys

engagement.login_days.count

engagement.login_streak_days

engagement.meaningful_sessions.count

1.2 “Meaningful messages” definition (anti-spam)
Rule
A message counts toward messaging goals only if it’s meaningful, defined as:

A message qualifies if any of the following:

≥ 25 characters OR

contains a Quick Action template OR

customer replies within 24 hours (reply validation)

Quick Action templates (recommended)
“On my way”

“I’ve arrived”

“Starting now”

“Finished — here are your photos”

“Any areas you want me to focus on?”

“I noticed {{issue}} — how would you like to proceed?”

Implementation
messages.sent_to_clients.meaningful.count

Messages used for goals should reference either template_id or meet char threshold

1.3 Photo verification quality rule (anti-gaming)
Rule
Photo goals should count only if:

includes both before and after sets

includes minimum N photos per set (default 3 before, 3 after)

timestamps must be within job window (clock-in/out time bounds)

optional: require 1 “wide shot” tag per set (later)

Implementation
jobs.photos.submission.valid.count

jobs.photos.approved.count (customer approved)

1.4 “Good Faith Declines” system (acceptance rate fairness)
Acceptance rate targets can feel coercive unless declines are fair.

Rule
A decline is penalty-free (doesn’t hurt acceptance rate) if the cleaner selects a good-faith reason.

Good Faith Decline reasons
Distance too far

Time conflict

Job type mismatch (deep / move-out not enabled)

Accessibility concern (stairs/heavy lift) — if cleaner opted out

Safety concern

Supplies requirement mismatch

Client notes conflict (pets/allergies) — if opted out

Limits (to prevent abuse)
Each cleaner gets X good-faith declines per 7 days (default 6)

Additional good-faith declines still count as declines (but logged)

Implementation
job_requests.declined.good_faith.count

job_requests.declined.count_total

job_requests.acceptance_rate_percent computed as:

accepts / (accepts + declines_non_good_faith)

1.5 Standardize ratings (reduce confusion)
Right now you have stars and percent in different levels. That’s confusing.

Decision (recommended)
Use stars everywhere in cleaner-facing UX.
Use “percent” internally only if needed.

Cleaner sees: 4.8 / 5

Admin sees: star avg + optional normalized score

Conversion (if you must keep percent)
rating_percent = (avg_stars / 5) * 100

But the UI should always show stars.

1.6 Define “On-time” + Clock-in/out precisely
On-time definition (default)
On-time if clock-in occurs within:

5 minutes early to 10 minutes late of scheduled start

Must be within GPS radius: 150 meters (configurable)

Late over 10 minutes
clock-in time > scheduled start + 10 minutes AND within GPS radius

Clock-in/out success
Clock-in AND clock-out recorded

Job duration not < 20 minutes (anti-fraud)

Must be within GPS radius at clock-in (and optionally clock-out)

2) Tier 2 Strongly Recommended (Revenue + dispute reduction)
2.1 Add revenue-quality lever: Add-ons (without being spammy)
Add-ons increase revenue per booking and cleaner earnings.

Add-on concept
Add-ons are customer-selected upgrades:

fridge, oven, laundry, windows, baseboards, etc.

Goal addition (suggested, mid levels)
Add a Stretch or Badge goal at Level 4–6:

“Complete 10 add-ons” (counts when customer selected add-on and cleaner completed it)

Metric
jobs.addons.completed.count

jobs.addons.attach_rate_percent (admin analytics)

Reward alignment
Non-cash:

“Access to add-on premium pool”

“Priority visibility to clients who buy add-ons”

Cash:

optional small milestone cash if margins allow

2.2 Dispute prevention micro-goals (teach habits, not just punish outcomes)
Add optional goals/badges that encode best practices:

Micro-goals / badges (non-economic or small boosts)
“Arrival confirmation” (used quick action “I’ve arrived”)

“Pre-clean checklist completed” (tap checklist)

“Issue flagged before completion” (creates a note + photo)

“Post-clean follow-up sent” (template message)

“Photos approved 10x” (already part of system)

These reduce disputes because customers feel informed and respected.

Metrics:

jobs.checklist.completed.count

jobs.issues.flagged_pre_completion.count

messages.templates.used.count

2.3 Expand choice rewards (reduce cost, increase perceived value)
Replace many “cash-only” rewards with a choose 1 menu.

Example menu (Level 5+)
Choose ONE:

$30 cash

Priority visibility 14 days

Implementation:

3) Tier 3 Nice-to-Have (Fun + engagement)
3.1 “Next Best Action” UX (prevents overwhelm)
Always show 1–2 actions with the highest expected impact:

closest-to-complete core goal

or stretch goal with biggest reward

API:

GET /cleaners/:id/progression returns next_best_actions[]

Logic:

prioritize goals with smallest remaining delta

boost goals with expiring season multipliers

3.2 Seasonal challenges (no resets)
Create time-limited events that modify progress counting.

Examples:

“Weekend Warrior Month: weekend jobs count 2× toward weekend goals”

“5⭐ Sprint: earn 3 five-stars this month → pick a reward”

“Deep Clean Season: deep cleans give bonus progress on Level 4 core”

Implementation:

season_rules table with:

start/end

eligibility

multiplier rules per metric

3.3 Badge layer (non-economic pride)
Badges cost nothing but improve retention.

Examples:

“First 5-star”

“Perfect Week”

“Move-out Specialist”

“Dispute-Free 60”

“Early Bird”

“Customer Favorite”

Badges are additive identity, not gating.

4) Marketplace Health Governor (Critical for scaling safely)
This is a regional “thermostat” that adjusts reward strength based on supply/demand.

Inputs (per region)
supply: active cleaners available

demand: job requests created

fill time: time to get booking accepted

cancellation rate

dispute rate

Outputs (config knobs)
visibility multipliers

early exposure minutes

cash rewards enable/disable by region

add-on reward emphasis

Example governor behaviors
If undersupply (jobs not filling)
increase early exposure windows

slightly increase visibility multipliers

reduce strictness on acceptance rate maintenance

temporarily increase “weekend/early” incentives

If oversupply (too many cleaners)
decrease multipliers slightly

increase emphasis on quality goals (ratings, disputes)

reduce cash rewards

keep visibility rewards but tighten eligibility

This prevents “one static system” from failing across markets.

1) QUICK TEMPLATES (UPDATED + EXPANDED)
These templates also solve “meaningful messages” (anti-spam) by letting messages count when sent via template.

Template List (v1)
1) Thank You
Key: tmpl_thank_you
Copy:

Thanks for choosing PureTask! I just finished up — let me know if there’s anything you’d like adjusted.

2) Would you like to reschedule?
Key: tmpl_reschedule_offer
Copy:

Hi! Would you like to reschedule this cleaning? If so, tell me a better day/time and I’ll confirm right away.

3) Request a review
Key: tmpl_review_request
Copy:

If you were happy with the cleaning, would you mind leaving a quick review? It really helps me get booked more often. Thank you!

4) Request a tip
Key: tmpl_tip_request
Copy:

If you feel I did a great job, tips are always appreciated — but never expected. Thank you for your support!

✅ Meaningful-message rule update:
A message counts if:

it’s sent using ANY template (template_id != null) OR

it’s ≥ 25 characters OR

the customer replies within 24h

2) PHOTO VERIFICATION RULE (SIMPLIFIED AS REQUESTED)
You’re right: we can’t “teach how to clean” by mandating angles/rooms.

New Photo Verification Quality Rule (v1)
A photo submission counts for photo-related goals if:

✅ At least 1 BEFORE photo AND 1 AFTER photo

✅ Both photos have timestamps that fall within the job’s active window:

after clock-in and before clock-out

✅ Photos are attached to the job

That’s it.

Metrics
jobs.photos.before.count >= 1

jobs.photos.after.count >= 1

jobs.photos.timestamp_valid.bool == true

All other constraints (min photo count, wide shot tags, etc.) are removed per your instruction.

3) GOOD FAITH DECLINES (EXPLICIT DEFINITIONS)
This is critical — it’s what makes acceptance goals feel fair at scale.

Good Faith Decline Reasons (v1) — with exact thresholds
A) Distance too far
Rule:
If the cleaner’s travel radius is 10 miles from home location, then:

Decline is good-faith if job distance ≥ 11.0 miles from the cleaner’s home base.

Metric support:

Store cleaner.home_location and cleaner.max_travel_miles (default 10)

Compute job.distance_from_home_miles

B) Time conflict (outside availability)
Rule:
Decline is good-faith if the job’s scheduled start time is outside the cleaner’s availability windows.

Examples:

Cleaner availability: Mon–Fri 9am–3pm

Job request: Tue 6pm → good-faith decline

Metric support:

cleaner.availability_windows

job.requested_start_time

C) Job mismatch (services not offered)
Decline is good-faith if the job requires something the cleaner has explicitly opted out of.

Examples:

Cleaner does not offer Move-in/Move-out

Client requests move-out cleaning → good-faith decline

Other examples:

Cleaner opts out of “pet homes”

Client indicates pets present → good-faith decline

Metric support:

cleaner.services_offered[]

job.required_services[]

cleaner.preferences[] (pets, stairs, heavy lifting, etc.)

D) Safety concern
Decline is good-faith if:

address flagged by platform, OR

client messages contain threatening/harassing behavior, OR

cleaner reports unsafe conditions (logged reason)

Guardrail: Require a reason detail text (min 20 chars) to avoid abuse.

E) Access / logistics mismatch
Decline is good-faith if:

building access instructions conflict with cleaner’s constraints (e.g., “must bring ladder,” “no parking,” etc.)
and the cleaner has that constraint marked.

F) Too short notice
Decline is good-faith if the job is created too close to the start time.

Rule (recommended):
If time_to_start < 3 hours → good-faith decline allowed.

(You can tune this later, but it’s important operationally.)

Limits to prevent abuse
6 good-faith declines per 7 days (default)

After that, good-faith declines still allowed but count toward acceptance-rate denominator.

Acceptance Rate Formula (Fair)
Acceptance rate uses only non-good-faith declines:

Acceptance Rate =
accepted / (accepted + declined_non_good_faith)

4) RATINGS STANDARDIZATION (CONFIRMED)
Cleaner-facing UX: stars only

“4.8 / 5”

Admin/internal: optional normalized percent

rating_percent = (avg_stars / 5) * 100

✅ Kept exactly as you approved.

5) ON-TIME + GPS (UPDATED TO YOUR VALUES)
On-time definition (v1)
A cleaner is “on-time” if:

clock-in occurs between:

15 minutes before start

15 minutes after start

AND clock-in GPS is within 250 meters of job location

Clock-in/out success (v1)
A job counts as “clock-in/out success” only if:

clock-in recorded within 250m

clock-out recorded within 250m

✅ removed the “job duration must be >20 minutes” rule per your instruction.

6) ADD-ONS — BOOKING FLOW + GOALS + REWARDS (CORE LEVELS 4/5/6)
You gave a very clear architecture. Here it is, fully specified.

6.1 When client selects add-ons (booking flow)
Add-ons happen during booking, after the client confirms expected hours.

Booking sequence (recommended)
Client selects cleaning type (basic/deep/move-out)

Client enters home info (beds/baths/notes)

Platform suggests a recommended hours range

Client confirms hours they want and will pay for

Client selects cleaner (or cleaner shortlist)

Add-ons step appears:

show add-ons supported by that cleaner

show add-on prices based on cleaner tier/reliability score

Checkout → credits escrowed

✅ This matches your intended model.

6.2 Cleaner add-on pricing rules
Each cleaner defines:

add-ons they offer

base add-on price
Platform applies tier multiplier based on reliability score.

Example internal:

addon_price = cleaner_base_addon_price * tier_multiplier

6.3 Add-on CORE goals (Levels 4/5/6)
Per your instruction: core goals, counts 30/45/60.

Level 4 Core
Goal: Complete 30 add-ons
Metric: jobs.addons.completed.count >= 30

Level 5 Core
Goal: Complete 45 add-ons
Metric: jobs.addons.completed.count >= 45

Level 6 Core
Goal: Complete 60 add-ons
Metric: jobs.addons.completed.count >= 60

6.4 Rewards for add-on mastery (I’m deciding per your request)
These rewards should:

increase bookings

increase high-value customers

not destroy margin

Level 4 add-on reward (after completing 30)
✅ “Add-on Spotlight” (14 days)

Cleaner is boosted specifically for customers selecting add-ons

Implementation: ranking multiplier applies only when job.has_addons = true

Level 5 add-on reward (after completing 45)
✅ “Add-on Preferred” (30 days)

Early exposure +10 minutes for add-on jobs

Visibility multiplier applies only to add-on jobs

Level 6 add-on reward (after completing 60)
✅ “Premium Add-on Access” (permanent permission)

Cleaner appears in a premium add-on recommended carousel

Unlocks higher add-on pricing ceiling (small, controlled)

This aligns incentives perfectly: better add-on execution → better add-on customers → more revenue for both sides.

8) SECTION 3.2 — SEASONAL CHALLENGES (WITH INTERNET CHECK + CITATIONS)
Here’s what demand tends to spike around:

Spring cleaning season tends to cluster across March–May. 

Moving season peaks May–September, which increases move-in/move-out cleaning demand. 

Holiday prep (Thanksgiving/December holidays) drives cleaning demand as people host gatherings. 

Seasonal Challenge Calendar (production-ready)
March–April: “Spring Refresh”
Deep cleans count 1.5× toward deep-clean goals

Badge: “Spring Refresh Pro”

May–September: “Move-Out Rush”
Move-in/out jobs count 2× toward move-in/out milestones

Add-on completion counts 1.25× (move-outs often include add-ons)

Badge: “Turnover Specialist”
(Aligned with moving peak season) 

Late Oct–Nov: “Holiday Prep”
Weekend jobs count 2× (hosts want weekends)

Review request templates unlock a small boost (non-cash)

Badge: “Holiday Hero” 

December: “End-of-Year Excellence”
Dispute-free streak rewards are emphasized

Bonus: “Customer Favorite” badge for 5-star streaks 

Fun-only challenges (your request)
These don’t depend on seasonality; they’re pure engagement:

“Weekend Warrior Month” — weekend jobs count 2×

“5-Star Sprint” — get 5 five-stars in 30 days → choice reward

“Perfect Week” — 7 days no lateness/cancel/dispute → badge + visibility boost

safety_reports.note_length

improved trust & moderation ability

minimal friction

avoids punishing people who can’t safely take photos

the job start time is less than 18 hours away from the time the request is sent.

Why this is good

prevents forcing cleaners into unsafe/rushed scheduling

reduces late arrivals + cancellations

improves reliability stats platform-wide

PURETASK CLEANER PROGRESSION & INCENTIVE SYSTEM
Final Canonical Specification (v1.0)

1. CORE PHILOSOPHY (LOCKED)
Cleaners never lose levels

Levels represent earned trust and access

Rewards accelerate earnings, they do not guarantee jobs

Customers always choose their cleaner

Visibility and priority are relative, not exclusive

Progression rewards behavior that:

increases platform revenue

reduces disputes

improves reliability and customer satisfaction

Maintenance rules pause rewards and progression, never revoke identity

2. LEVEL SYSTEM STRUCTURE (LEVELS 1–10)
Each level contains three components:

A) Core Goals — Required
Must complete all

Define minimum professional standards for that level

B) Stretch Goals — Choice
Must complete at least one

Multiple paths to success (volume, quality, timing, loyalty, etc.)

C) Maintenance Rules — Ongoing
Must remain compliant

Failure pauses rewards/progress (never levels)

Leveling Up
Leveling up itself has minimal reward

The real reward = access to bigger goals and bigger rewards

Progression can pause but never reverses

3. GLOBAL GOAL COVERAGE (CONFIRMED)
The goal system explicitly and intentionally covers every major PureTask surface:

Booking & job completion

Job acceptance & responsiveness

Clock-in / clock-out

Messaging & communication

Photo verification

Ratings & reviews

Dispute prevention

Availability discipline

Repeat clients

Premium job types (deep / move-out)

High-demand time slots (weekend / early)

Long-term reliability

Login & engagement

Platform rules & compliance

Add-ons & revenue quality

Result:
No cleaner can “game” PureTask by using only one feature.
Progression requires full-system mastery.

4. DEFINITIVE METRIC RULES (ANTI-GAMING)
4.1 Meaningful Login (Anti-Streak Abuse)
A “login day” counts only if at least one meaningful action is performed within 15 minutes:

Counts as meaningful:

Open a job request

Accept or decline a job

Send a meaningful message

Confirm on-the-way / arrived / completed

Upload photos

Update availability

Does NOT count:

App open + close

Passive notification taps

4.2 Meaningful Messages (Anti-Spam)
A message counts if any of the following are true:

Sent using a Quick Template

≥ 25 characters

Customer replies within 24 hours

Quick Templates (Final Set)
On my way

I’ve arrived

Starting now

Finished — photos attached

Any focus areas?

Thank you

Would you like to reschedule?

Request a review

Request a tip

4.3 Photo Verification (Simplified & Locked)
A job satisfies photo requirements if:

At least 1 before photo

At least 1 after photo

Photos are timestamped between clock-in and clock-out

No room requirements.
No angle requirements.
No instructional cleaning bias.

4.4 On-Time Definition (Final)
A job is “on time” if:

Clock-in occurs 15 minutes before to 15 minutes after scheduled start

Clock-in GPS is within 250 meters of job location

4.5 Clock-In / Clock-Out Success (Final)
Counts only if:

Clock-in within 250m

Clock-out within 250m

(No minimum duration rule.)

5. GOOD FAITH DECLINES (FAIR ACCEPTANCE SYSTEM)
Declines that meet these rules do not hurt acceptance rate.

A) Distance Too Far
Cleaner travel radius = 10 miles

Job ≥ 11 miles from home base → good-faith decline

B) Time Conflict
Job start time outside cleaner’s availability windows

C) Job Mismatch
Cleaner does not offer requested service
(e.g., move-in/out, pets, stairs, heavy lifting)

D) Safety Concern (Updated)
Platform-flagged address OR

Harassing/threatening client behavior OR

Cleaner reports unsafe conditions

Optional: upload 1 photo (not required)
If no photo → short written explanation required

E) Access / Logistics Mismatch
Client requirements conflict with cleaner’s listed constraints

F) Short Notice (Updated)
Job start time is less than 18 hours from request time

Limits
6 good-faith declines per 7 days

After limit, declines still allowed but count toward acceptance rate

Acceptance Rate Formula
accepted / (accepted + declined_non_good_faith)
6. RATINGS SYSTEM (STANDARDIZED)
Cleaner-facing UX: stars only
Example: 4.8 / 5

Internal/Admin: stars + optional normalized percent

7. ADD-ONS SYSTEM (REVENUE QUALITY LEVER)
7.1 When Add-ons Are Selected
During booking, after:

Client estimates hours they’re willing to pay for

Client selects a cleaner

Client then sees:

Add-ons that cleaner offers

Add-on prices based on cleaner reliability tier

7.2 Cleaner Add-on Pricing
Cleaner sets base add-on prices

Platform applies tier multiplier

Higher reliability = higher ceiling

7.3 Add-on Core Goals (Locked)
Level	Core Goal
Level 4	Complete 30 add-ons
Level 5	Complete 45 add-ons
Level 6	Complete 60 add-ons
7.4 Add-on Rewards (Final)
Level 4: Add-on Spotlight (14 days)
→ boosted visibility only for add-on jobs

Level 5: Add-on Preferred (30 days)
→ early exposure + visibility for add-on jobs

Level 6: Premium Add-on Access (permanent)
→ premium add-on carousel + higher pricing ceiling

8. BEST PRACTICES SECTION (NON-GATING)
Purpose:

Reduce disputes

Improve professionalism

Educate without micromanaging

Includes:

Arrival confirmation

Completion follow-up

Early issue flagging

Respectful communication

Photo submission habits

May unlock badges, not progression.

9. SEASONAL & FUN CHALLENGES (NO RESETS)
Demand-Aligned Seasons
Spring Refresh (Mar–Apr): deep cleans boosted

Move-Out Rush (May–Sep): move-in/out boosted

Holiday Prep (Nov): weekend demand boosted

End-of-Year Excellence (Dec): dispute-free emphasis

Fun Challenges
Weekend Warrior Month

5-Star Sprint

Perfect Week

Add-On Hustle

Levels remain permanent.
Only rewards rotate.

10. BADGE SYSTEM (FINAL, REDUCED & MEANINGFUL)

11. VISIBILITY & PRIORITY (SCALED CORRECTLY)
Visibility affects ordering, not eligibility

Applies only within:

location

availability

job type

Boosts are temporary and conditional

Customers can always scroll and choose freely

12. MAINTENANCE RULES (CONTROL WITHOUT PUNISHMENT)
Maintenance failures:

pause rewards

pause progression

Never remove levels

Always show:

what failed

how to recover

This is a behavioral governor, not a hammer.

13. ADMIN CONTROL SYSTEM (FINAL)
Admins can:

Tune goals (thresholds, windows)

Enable/disable rewards

Cap cash burn

Adjust visibility multipliers

Manage seasonal challenges

Operate regional Marketplace Health Governor

Pause rewards for abuse (never levels)

Audit & rollback changes

14. MARKETPLACE HEALTH GOVERNOR
Per region:

Adjust reward strength based on supply/demand

Emphasize quality in oversupply

Emphasize access in undersupply

Keeps system stable at scale

15. FINAL VERDICT
You now have:

A complete, defensible, scalable cleaner incentive system

One that:

drives revenue

reduces disputes

rewards effort fairly

feels motivating and professional

cannot be easily gamed

scales city by city

This is not MVP-level.
This is best-in-class marketplace infrastructure.

> Scope: Implement the complete Cleaner Progression & Incentive System spec (Levels 1–10, goals, rewards, badges, seasonal challenges, admin controls, governor, anti-gaming, and metrics contract).

## EPIC A — Data Model & Config Versioning

- **Description:** Add Postgres tables for:
  - `gamification_config_versions`
  - `goal_definitions`
  - `level_definitions`
  - `reward_definitions`
  - `badge_definitions`
  - `season_definitions`
  - `region_governor_configs`
