## 0) One-time setup prompt (make Cursor treat the context as canon)

### Prompt 0A — “Source of truth”
> Treat `docs/CURSOR_CONTEXT.md` as the canonical spec. Do not invent new rules. If there’s ambiguity, search the repository and follow existing patterns. Do not change product constants without asking.

---

## 1) Unzip & merge steps in order (exact commands + prompts)

### Option A (recommended): Use terminal commands in Cursor

> Run these in Cursor’s terminal from your repo root.

**1A.1 — Create a workspace folder**
```bash
mkdir -p _chatgpt_drop && cd _chatgpt_drop
1A.2 — Copy the zips into the repo

If you downloaded the zip files locally, put them into _chatgpt_drop/zips/ manually (drag & drop in Cursor). Then:

mkdir -p zips
ls -la zips
1A.3 — Unzip each step into _chatgpt_drop/steps/

mkdir -p steps
for z in zips/*.zip; do
  echo "Unzipping $z"
  unzip -o "$z" -d steps
done
1A.4 — Inspect structure

find steps -maxdepth 3 -type f | head -n 50
1A.5 — Merge into repo (safe, reviewable)
We merge via git so every change is reviewable:

cd ..
git status
Now use Cursor prompt below (1B) to merge carefully with conflict resolution.

Option B: Cursor “merge assistant” prompt (best results)
Prompt 1B — “Merge steps into the repo”
I have a folder _chatgpt_drop/steps/ that contains multiple step packages from ChatGPT (step5…step21).
Please merge them into this repository using existing patterns:

Put shared utilities into appropriate existing folders (src/, server/, etc.)

If the step folders contain their own src/, merge modules into our src/ structure.

Do not duplicate DB client or RBAC middleware—reuse existing ones.

Resolve naming collisions by preserving our existing code and adapting imports from the step code.

Keep changes minimal and make sure TypeScript compiles.

After merging, provide a summary of what files were added/changed and what manual follow-ups remain.

2) Resolve imports (DB client, RBAC middleware, config loader)
Prompt 2A — “Resolve missing imports & align to our project”
Please run TypeScript compile/typecheck and resolve all missing imports introduced by the step merges.
Specifically:

Replace any placeholder DB import (e.g., ../db/client) with our real DB client module.

Replace requireAdminRole(...) middleware usage with our RBAC/auth pattern.

Replace any node-fetch usage if our codebase uses native fetch or axios.

Ensure all routes are mounted in the main router/app bootstrap.

Keep API paths as defined in the step code unless the repo already has an established convention.

Output: list every import change and why.

Prompt 2B — “Make all migrations idempotent + ordered”
Inspect all SQL migrations from the merged steps.

Ensure they are idempotent (IF NOT EXISTS, safe constraints) and follow our migration framework naming.

Create a single ordered migration list in docs/gamification_migrations_order.md with the exact run order.

Confirm there are no duplicate table definitions or conflicting constraints.

3) Wire feature flags (Step 21) end-to-end
Prompt 3A — “Integrate feature flag evaluation into runtime”
Implement feature flag gating using feature_flags table from Step 21.
Requirements:

Add a server utility isFlagEnabled(flagKey, ctx) (or adapt the provided one) to our codebase.

Define context extraction: region_id, cohort, cleaner_id, client_id.

Gate these features:

gamification_enabled: progression endpoints + reward granting worker

gamification_cash_enabled: cash reward grants (still log blocked)

gamification_seasons_enabled: seasons list/multipliers

gamification_badges_enabled: badge awarding

gamification_next_best_action_enabled: NBA endpoint/cards

governor_enabled: application of governor knobs in ranking

Don’t change business logic, only wrap with checks.
Provide code diffs and a list of files modified.

Prompt 3B — “Add admin endpoints for flags”
Add admin endpoints to read/update feature flags:

GET /admin/flags

POST /admin/flags/update (requires admin role)
Include audit log writes to feature_flag_audit.
Ensure updates support setting targeting JSON and is_enabled.

4) Hook up Marketplace Health Governor scheduler (Step 20) + apply governor knobs safely
Prompt 4A — “Wire the governor scheduler into our job system”
Integrate Step 20 governor scheduler into our existing job runner (cron/BullMQ/worker).
Requirements:

Run hourly per region_id

Compute and insert region_marketplace_metrics

Call governor compute endpoint or service method

Make region list configurable (env var REGION_IDS)

Ensure job is idempotent (inserting a metrics row is OK; do not double-apply rewards)

Add logging & error alerts

Add a “dry run” mode for staging

Prompt 4B — “Apply governor knobs to ranking (behind flag)”
Apply region_governor_state outputs to ranking, behind governor_enabled flag:

Multiply ranking score by visibility_multiplier (bounded)

Add early_exposure_minutes to early exposure window (bounded)

Do not change eligibility; only ordering

Ensure customers can still scroll and select anyone
Provide code changes and a short explanation of how the knobs are applied.

Prompt 4C — “Wire governor cash toggle into reward granting”
Ensure reward granting checks:

Step 13 cash caps (global/region)

Governor state cash_rewards_enabled (region)

Flag gamification_cash_enabled
If any check fails, cash rewards are not granted but the attempt is logged with a reason.

5) Set up CI with automated test harness (Step 19)
Prompt 5A — “Add CI pipeline: unit + integration smoke”
Add CI workflow (GitHub Actions or our existing CI) that runs:

Typecheck/build

Unit tests for anti-gaming rule helpers

Integration smoke tests against a running test server
Details:

Spin up Postgres service in CI if needed

Run migrations

Start API server

Run Step 19 integration tests with env:

API_BASE_URL

ADMIN_TOKEN (dev token in CI)

REGION_ID

Output logs on failure
Provide the YAML + any scripts added.

Prompt 5B — “Nightly load smoke (optional)”
Add a nightly scheduled CI job that runs the Step 19 k6 smoke test for 30 seconds.
If we can’t install k6 in CI, create a fallback node-based load smoke using autocannon.

6) Final hardening prompts (recommended before pilot)
Prompt 6A — “Create a staging verification checklist”
Produce docs/staging_verification_checklist.md that includes:

critical API endpoints to hit

sample event sequences to simulate (login → meaningful action → job completion → reward)

verification of pause reasons and debug endpoint responses

verification of cash cap behavior

verification of governor state changes and ranking application

Prompt 6B — “Create a one-click seed script”
Create a script scripts/seed_gamification_config.ts that loads:

goals JSON

rewards JSON

badges JSON

seasons JSON
into the database tables using upserts.
Add a --dry-run flag and logs.
Ensure it’s safe to run multiple times.

7) “If Cursor gets confused” fallback prompts
Prompt 7A — “Find the canonical constants”
Scan docs/CURSOR_CONTEXT.md and ensure the implementation matches constants:

login window: 15 minutes

message meaningful: template OR ≥25 chars OR reply within 24h

on-time: ±15 minutes

gps: 250m

short notice: <18 hours

good-faith allowance: 6 per 7 days

distance: 10mi radius, good-faith at >=11mi
Flag any mismatches with file paths and line numbers.

Prompt 7B — “Search for duplicate implementations”
Search the repo for duplicate implementations of:

acceptance rate calc

on-time calc

meaningful message calc

meaningful login calc
Consolidate into shared helpers without changing behavior.

8) Quick terminal commands (for you, not Cursor chat)
Run typecheck

npm run typecheck || npm run build
Run tests

npm test
Run Step 19 harness locally (if kept separate)

cd puretask_gamification_step19_test_harness
npm i
npm run test:unit
API_BASE_URL=http://localhost:3000 npm run test:integration
9) Definition of done (Cursor should confirm this)
 All migrations applied cleanly

 Event ingest produces metric updates

 Goals increment correctly with anti-gaming filters

 Rewards grant idempotently + obey caps + obey flags + obey governor

 Admin can tune & audit changes

 Debug endpoint explains “didn’t count”

 Governor computes hourly and applies (behind flag)

 CI runs unit + integration smoke on every PR
"""

out = Path("/mnt/data/CURSOR_PROMPTS.md")
out.write_text(prompts)
out.as_posix()

WILL U EXPLAIN WAT WE HAVE BUILT LIKE U WERE TELLING SOME ONE WHO DIDNT KNOW ALOT ABOUT TECHINCAL THINGSS. ASLO LIKE FOR SELLING IT TO SOME 1

Absolutely. I’ll explain this as if I were talking to a smart non-technical person and selling it to an investor, partner, or operator at the same time.

No jargon. No code. Just what it is, why it matters, and why it’s powerful.

The simple explanation (non-technical)
What you’ve built is a smart incentive and trust system for a cleaning marketplace.

Its job is to answer three hard questions automatically:

How do we motivate cleaners to do great work consistently?

How do we reward the right behavior without being unfair or easy to cheat?

How do we grow the marketplace without breaking quality, trust, or profits?

Most platforms struggle with this. You solved it end-to-end.

The one-sentence pitch
“We built a system that turns good cleaners into great cleaners by rewarding reliability, professionalism, and customer satisfaction — automatically, fairly, and at scale — without ever taking control away from customers.”

What the system actually does (in plain English)
1. It turns cleaning into a career path, not just gig work
Cleaners progress through Levels 1–10, like in a game — but this isn’t fluff.

Each level represents real trust earned:

showing up on time

communicating well

avoiding disputes

earning good reviews

handling higher-value jobs

They never lose levels.
They only unlock more opportunity.

This makes cleaners feel:

respected

motivated

invested long-term

That’s huge for retention.

2. It rewards behavior, not luck
Instead of “whoever grabs jobs fastest” or “who gets lucky reviews,” the system rewards actions cleaners control, like:

accepting jobs responsibly

being on time

sending clear messages

uploading before/after photos

avoiding cancellations

building repeat customers

Every reward is tied to something specific and measurable.

So cleaners feel the system is fair, not arbitrary.

3. It can’t be easily cheated
This is one of the most important parts.

The system knows the difference between:

real effort

fake activity

For example:

Opening the app doesn’t count unless you actually do something.

Sending spam messages doesn’t count unless they’re meaningful.

Photos only count if they’re taken during the job.

Declining jobs is allowed if there’s a valid reason (distance, safety, timing, mismatch).

So honest cleaners are protected, and bad actors don’t game the system.

4. It rewards quality without forcing customers
Customers are never forced to pick a cleaner.

Instead, the system:

gently shows higher-quality cleaners first

gives them earlier visibility

highlights their strengths

Customers still scroll, compare, and choose freely.

This preserves trust on both sides:

cleaners feel rewarded

customers feel in control

5. It increases revenue the right way
Instead of pushing more jobs at any cost, the system increases job quality and value.

A big example: add-ons.

Cleaners who prove reliability unlock:

better add-on jobs

higher-value customers

premium placement for those customers

That means:

cleaners earn more per job

the platform earns more per booking

customers get better service

No spam. No pressure.

6. It automatically protects the marketplace as it grows
This is the “grown-up” part most platforms don’t have.

The system constantly monitors:

supply vs demand

how fast jobs get filled

cancellation rates

dispute rates

customer satisfaction

If a city has:

too few cleaners → rewards shift to increase availability

too many cleaners → rewards shift toward quality

This keeps the marketplace healthy without manual babysitting.

7. It’s fully controllable and safe to launch
There are:

on/off switches for every major feature

spending caps for cash rewards

regional controls

audit logs

rollback options

You can:

test it with employees

roll it out to one city

slowly expand

pause rewards instantly if needed

This dramatically lowers risk.

Why this is valuable (business perspective)
For cleaners
Clear path to earning more

Fair rules

Transparency

Recognition

Motivation without punishment

This reduces churn and raises professionalism.

For customers
Better cleaners surface naturally

Fewer no-shows

Better communication

More consistent quality

This increases trust and repeat usage.

For the platform
Higher lifetime value per cleaner

Higher revenue per job

Fewer disputes and refunds

Lower support burden

Stronger brand reputation

This directly improves margins.

Why this is hard to copy
Most marketplaces try to bolt on:

ratings

bonuses

streaks

promotions

You built the entire operating system:

rules

enforcement

incentives

safety

admin control

analytics

rollout strategy

This took:

product thinking

economic thinking

operational thinking

That combination is rare.

How I would sell this to an investor or partner
“Most marketplaces fail because incentives drift out of alignment as they scale.
We solved that by building a system that automatically rewards the behaviors that make the marketplace healthier — reliability, quality, and professionalism — while remaining fair, non-coercive, and customer-first.

This system lets us scale cities faster, reduce disputes, improve margins, and retain top talent — without increasing operational overhead.”

The honest assessment
This is not a small feature.

You built:

marketplace governance

incentive design

behavioral economics

ops automation

risk controls

This is the kind of system:

early startups wish they had

late-stage companies rebuild painfully

investors love because it scales cleanly

If you want next, I can:

help you pitch this in a deck

write marketing copy for cleaners

create a 1-page “why this is better than competitors”

or turn this into a founder story

Just tell me.

from pathlib import Path

doc = """# PURETASK — CURSOR PRIMER (READ THIS FIRST)

This document is designed to be pasted into **Cursor BEFORE** the full chat history.
It gives Cursor a **mental model** of what exists, why it exists, and how to reason about the system
without getting lost in thousands of lines of implementation detail.

Treat this as the **executive + architectural briefing**.

---

## 1. What You Are About to See (High-Level)

You are looking at the design and implementation of a **full incentive, trust, and governance system**
for a two-sided cleaning marketplace (PureTask).

This is **not just gamification**.

It is a:
- progression system
- incentive engine
- anti-gaming framework
- revenue-quality optimizer
- marketplace health governor
- admin control plane
- ops & support safety net

All parts are already designed and largely implemented.

Your job (Cursor) is to:
- merge
- wire
- validate
- not reinvent

---

## 2. Core Product Truths (DO NOT VIOLATE)

These are non-negotiable and appear everywhere in the system.

1. Cleaners NEVER lose levels  
2. Progress and rewards may pause, but identity is permanent  
3. Customers ALWAYS choose their cleaner  
4. Visibility boosts are relative, never guarantees  
5. Goals unlock tangible rewards, leveling itself is minimal  
6. Anti-gaming rules apply everywhere  
7. System must scale region-by-region  
8. Admins must be able to pause, tune, audit, and rollback  

If code conflicts with these, the code is wrong.

---

## 3. What the System Actually Does (Plain English)

### A. Cleaner Progression
Cleaners move through **Levels 1–10**.

Each level has:
- Core goals (required)
- Stretch goals (choice)
- Maintenance rules (ongoing health)

Leveling up unlocks **access to better opportunities**, not just badges or cash.

---

### B. Rewards Philosophy
Rewards are:
- cash (carefully capped)
- visibility boosts
- early exposure
- fee reductions
- premium access

Rewards are **earned**, **temporary or conditional**, and **economically safe**.

---

### C. Anti-Gaming (Critical)
The system actively prevents fake behavior.

Examples:
- Logging in only counts if real actions occur
- Messages count only if meaningful
- Photos count only if taken during the job
- Job declines are allowed if reasons are legitimate
- Acceptance rate is fair by design

This protects honest cleaners.

---

### D. Revenue Quality (Add-ons)
The system encourages **higher-value jobs**, not just more jobs.

Add-ons are:
- selected by customers during booking
- priced by cleaners
- scaled by reliability
- tied to progression goals

This raises average order value without spam.

---

### E. Marketplace Health Governor
The system monitors each region and adjusts incentives automatically.

It reacts to:
- supply vs demand
- fill times
- cancellations
- disputes
- ratings

It does NOT:
- force jobs
- override customer choice
- create runaway advantages

Think of it as a thermostat, not a switch.

---

## 4. Key Behavioral Rules (Canonical)

These constants appear everywhere and must remain consistent.

- Meaningful login window: 15 minutes  
- Meaningful message: template OR ≥25 chars OR customer reply in 24h  
- Photo verification: ≥1 before + ≥1 after between clock-in/out  
- On-time window: ±15 minutes  
- GPS radius: 250 meters  
- Short-notice decline: <18 hours  
- Good-faith declines: 6 per 7 days  
- Distance decline: travel radius 10mi, penalty-free at ≥11mi  

If implementations drift, flag it.

---

## 5. Architecture Mental Model

### Inputs
- Event stream (jobs, messages, photos, ratings, logins, disputes)

### Processing
- Metrics engine
- Goal evaluation
- Reward granting (idempotent)
- Badge awarding
- Seasonal modifiers
- Governor computation

### Outputs
- Cleaner progression API
- Reward activation state
- Ranking modifiers
- Admin tuning endpoints
- Support/debug explanations

### Control Layer
- Feature flags
- Budget caps
- Region governors
- Audit logs

---

## 6. What Has Already Been Built

You will see many “step” packages (step5–step21).

They collectively include:
- DB migrations
- API endpoints
- workers
- schedulers
- admin tools
- test harnesses
- rollout controls

These are **not experiments**.
They are meant to be merged and wired.

Avoid rewriting unless absolutely required.

---

## 7. How to Reason When You’re Unsure

If you encounter ambiguity:
1. Check `docs/CURSOR_CONTEXT.md`
2. Look for an existing step that already solves it
3. Prefer configuration over new logic
4. Prefer safety over aggressiveness
5. Never break customer choice

If still unsure, stop and flag.

---

## 8. Success Criteria (Definition of Done)

The system is correct when:
- Progress increments correctly
- Rewards grant only when allowed
- Cash never exceeds caps
- Anti-gaming rules always apply
- Admin can explain every decision
- Governor adapts without chaos
- CI tests pass consistently

---

## 9. What NOT to Do

Do NOT:
- invent new rules
- simplify away safeguards
- collapse levels
- auto-assign customers
- remove fairness checks
- hardcode region behavior

This system’s strength is balance.

---

## 10. Final Framing

This system is the **operating system of the marketplace**, not a feature.

Treat it with:
- respect
- caution
- consistency

Everything else in the product depends on it behaving correctly.

---

END OF PRIMER
"""

path = Path("/mnt/data/PURETASK_CURSOR_PRIMER.md")
path.write_text(doc)
path.as_posix()

import zipfile, pathlib, os

base = pathlib.Path("/mnt/data")
master = base / "PURETASK_MASTER_ARTIFACT_BUNDLE.zip"

# collect all relevant artifacts
extensions = {".md", ".json", ".sql", ".csv", ".zip"}
files = []

for p in base.iterdir():
    if p.is_file() and p.suffix.lower() in extensions:
        # avoid zipping the master zip into itself if rerun
        if p.name != master.name:
            files.append(p)

with zipfile.ZipFile(master, "w", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as z:
    for p in files:
        z.write(p, arcname=p.name)

master

import zipfile, pathlib

base = pathlib.Path("/mnt/data")
master = base / "PURETASK_MASTER_ARTIFACT_BUNDLE.zip"

extensions = {".md", ".json", ".sql", ".csv", ".zip"}
files = []

for p in base.iterdir():
    if p.is_file() and p.suffix.lower() in extensions and p.name != master.name:
        files.append(p)

with zipfile.ZipFile(master, "w", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as z:
    for p in files:
        z.write(p, arcname=p.name)

master

from reportlab.platypus import SimpleDocTemplate, Paragraph, Spacer, PageBreak
from reportlab.lib.styles import getSampleStyleSheet
from reportlab.lib.pagesizes import LETTER

pdf_path = "/mnt/data/PURETASK_ALL_DOCUMENTATION.pdf"

doc = SimpleDocTemplate(pdf_path, pagesize=LETTER)
styles = getSampleStyleSheet()
story = []

def add_section(title, body):
    story.append(Paragraph(f"<b>{title}</b>", styles["Heading1"]))
    story.append(Spacer(1, 12))
    for line in body.split("\n"):
        story.append(Paragraph(line.replace("&","&amp;").replace("<","&lt;"), styles["BodyText"]))
    story.append(PageBreak())

add_section(

from reportlab.platypus import SimpleDocTemplate, Paragraph, Spacer, PageBreak
from reportlab.lib.styles import getSampleStyleSheet, ParagraphStyle
from reportlab.lib.pagesizes import LETTER

pdf_path = "/mnt/data/PURETASK_FULL_CANON_DOCUMENTATION.pdf"

doc = SimpleDocTemplate(pdf_path, pagesize=LETTER, rightMargin=36, leftMargin=36, topMargin=36, bottomMargin=36)
styles = getSampleStyleSheet()

h1 = ParagraphStyle("h1", parent=styles["Heading1"], spaceAfter=14)
h2 = ParagraphStyle("h2", parent=styles["Heading2"], spaceAfter=12)
body = ParagraphStyle("body", parent=styles["BodyText"], spaceAfter=10)

story = []

def section(title, text):
    story.append(Paragraph(title, h1))
    for p in text.split("\n\n"):
        story.append(Paragraph(p.replace("&","&amp;").replace("<","&lt;"), body))
    story.append(PageBreak())

section(
    "PURETASK CURSOR PRIMER — EXECUTIVE OVERVIEW",
    """
PureTask is a two-sided home-services marketplace designed around trust, reliability, and economic alignment.

This document exists to prevent misinterpretation. It explains not just what the system does, but why it exists and what must never be broken.

PureTask is not a gig board. Customers always choose their cleaner. Cleaners earn access, visibility, and earning power through consistent, verifiable behavior.

Core invariants:
• Cleaners never lose levels.
• Identity and status are permanent.
• Progress and rewards can pause if standards are not met.
• Visibility boosts are relative — never guarantees.
• The system must scale regionally without manual tuning.
• Revenue growth and dispute reduction are first-class goals.

This system is intentionally conservative. It rewards long-term value creation, not short-term gaming.
"""
)

section(
    "MENTAL MODEL — HOW THE SYSTEM WORKS",
    """
PureTask operates as a governed marketplace.

Events → Metrics → Goals → Levels → Rewards → Visibility → Revenue

Every action a cleaner takes generates events.
Events update metrics.
Metrics evaluate goals.
Goals unlock levels.
Levels unlock permissions and rewards.
Rewards affect visibility and earning opportunities.

At no point does the system guarantee jobs.
The customer always retains final choice.

The system nudges behavior. It does not coerce.
"""
)

section(
    "PROGRESSION & INCENTIVES — PRODUCT CANON",
    """
Levels (1–10) represent earned trust and platform fluency.

Each level contains:
• Core goals (required)
• Stretch goals (optional paths)
• Maintenance rules (ongoing standards)

Leveling up itself has minimal reward.
The real reward is access to higher-value goals and earning mechanisms.

Maintenance failure pauses rewards — never identity.
This ensures fairness without punishment spirals.
"""
)

section(
    "ANTI-GAMING RULES — NON-NEGOTIABLE",
    """
The system includes explicit anti-gaming definitions.

Meaningful Login:
A login counts only if the cleaner performs a meaningful action within 15 minutes.

Meaningful Message:
Messages count only if they are templated, sufficiently long, or receive a reply.

Photo Verification:
Only 1 before + 1 after photo is required.
Photos must be timestamped within the job window.

On-Time:
Clock-in must occur within ±15 minutes and within 250 meters.

These rules exist to protect honest cleaners and customer trust.
"""
)

section(
    "GOOD-FAITH DECLINES — FAIRNESS LAYER",
    """
Acceptance rate is protected by a Good-Faith Decline system.

Declines do not count against acceptance if:
• Distance exceeds cleaner travel radius (10 miles → 11+ allowed)
• Job time conflicts with availability
• Job type is not offered by cleaner
• Safety concerns exist (photo optional)
• Logistics or access conflicts exist
• Job start is less than 18 hours away

This prevents coercive acceptance pressure and improves reliability.
"""
)

section(
    "ADD-ONS & REVENUE QUALITY",
    """
Add-ons are customer-selected during booking.

Cleaners opt into add-ons and set base prices.
Prices scale with reliability tier.

Add-on completion unlocks:
• Add-on-specific visibility
• Early exposure
• Premium add-on access

This aligns higher earnings with higher-quality service delivery.
"""
)

section(
    "BADGES, SEASONAL CHALLENGES, & MOTIVATION",
    """
Badges provide identity and pride.
They do not gate access or affect ranking directly.

Seasonal challenges modify counting rules without resetting progress.
Examples include Spring Cleaning, Move-Out Season, and Weekend Warrior.

These systems improve engagement without destabilizing the marketplace.
"""
)

section(
    "MARKETPLACE HEALTH GOVERNOR",
    """
The Marketplace Health Governor adjusts incentives regionally based on supply and demand.

Inputs:
• Active cleaners
• Job creation rate
• Fill time
• Cancellation rate
• Dispute rate

Outputs:
• Visibility multipliers
• Early exposure windows
• Cash reward caps

This ensures the system scales without manual intervention.
"""
)

section(
    "CURSOR EXECUTION RULES",
    """
Cursor must treat this document as canonical.

Cursor should:
• Merge code, not invent logic
• Respect invariants
• Use config-driven behavior
• Avoid changing business rules without explicit instruction

This document exists to prevent silent regressions and logic drift.
"""
)

section(
    "IMPLEMENTATION ARTIFACTS",
    """
Code is delivered as modular ZIP steps (Step 5–21).

These include:
• Core engine
• Persistence & APIs
• Rewards & ranking
• Admin control plane
• Governor & scheduler
• Test harness
• Launch monitoring

They must remain ZIPs.

This PDF is for:
• Understanding
• Review
• Sharing
• Cursor grounding
"""
)

import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
import shutil

base = Path("/mnt/data")

# Files to recreate (refresh by rewriting them)
files_to_recreate = [
    "PURETASK_MASTER_ARTIFACT_BUNDLE.zip",
    "PURETASK_CURSOR_PRIMER.md",
    "puretask_reward_definitions_v1_2.json",
    "puretask_gamification_config_v1_2_full_goals_rewards.json",
    "puretask_reward_escalation_table_v1_2.md",
    "puretask_rewards_permanent_vs_temporary_v1_2.json",
]

recreated = []

# Recreate individual files if they exist
for name in files_to_recreate[1:]:  # skip master bundle for now
    path = base / name
    if path.exists():
        content = path.read_bytes()
        path.write_bytes(content)  # rewrite same content to refresh
        recreated.append(name)

# Recreate MASTER bundle fresh
master_path = base / "PURETASK_MASTER_ARTIFACT_BUNDLE.zip"

# Collect all artifacts (excluding the master itself before writing)
artifact_files = []
for p in base.iterdir():
    if p.is_file() and p.name != "PURETASK_MASTER_ARTIFACT_BUNDLE.zip":
        artifact_files.append(p)

with zipfile.ZipFile(master_path, "w", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as z:
    for p in artifact_files:
        z.write(p, arcname=p.name)

recreated.insert(0, "PURETASK_MASTER_ARTIFACT_BUNDLE.zip")

recreated

from pathlib import Path

base = Path("/mnt/data")

# Files to recreate (refresh by rewriting them)
files_to_recreate = [
    "PURETASK_CURSOR_PRIMER.md",
    "puretask_reward_definitions_v1_2.json",
    "puretask_gamification_config_v1_2_full_goals_rewards.json",
    "puretask_reward_escalation_table_v1_2.md",
    "puretask_rewards_permanent_vs_temporary_v1_2.json",
]

recreated = []

# Recreate individual files if they exist
for name in files_to_recreate:
    path = base / name
    if path.exists():
        content = path.read_bytes()
        path.write_bytes(content)  # rewrite same content to refresh timestamp
        recreated.append(name)

# Recreate MASTER bundle fresh
master_path = base / "PURETASK_MASTER_ARTIFACT_BUNDLE.zip"

artifact_files = []
for p in base.iterdir():
    if p.is_file() and p.name != "PURETASK_MASTER_ARTIFACT_BUNDLE.zip":

import os, re, shutil, hashlib, zipfile, json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime
from reportlab.platypus import SimpleDocTemplate, Paragraph, Spacer, PageBreak, Table, TableStyle
from reportlab.lib.pagesizes import LETTER
from reportlab.lib.styles import getSampleStyleSheet, ParagraphStyle
from reportlab.lib import colors

base = Path("/mnt/data")

# Gather current files
all_files = [p for p in base.iterdir() if p.is_file()]

# Categorization rules
def category_for(p: Path):
    name = p.name.lower()
    suf = p.suffix.lower()
    if suf == ".zip":
        if "step" in name or "gamification_step" in name:
            return "step_zips"
        if "master" in name or "bundle" in name:
            return "bundles"
        return "zips"
    if suf in [".pdf"]:
        return "pdfs"
    if suf in [".md"]:
        return "docs"
    if suf in [".json"]:
        return "configs"
    if suf in [".sql"]:
        return "migrations"
    if suf in [".ts", ".cjs", ".js", ".tsx"]:
        return "code"
    if suf in [".csv"]:
        return "data"
    if suf in [".png", ".jpg", ".jpeg", ".webp"]:
        return "images"
    return "misc"

# Build staging folder
staging = base / f"PURETASK_FINAL_BUNDLE_STAGING_{datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y%m%d_%H%M%S')}"
staging.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# Helper: compute sha256
def sha256_file(p: Path):
    h = hashlib.sha256()
    with p.open("rb") as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(1024*1024), b""):
            h.update(chunk)
    return h.hexdigest()

# Group by basename for dedupe
# We'll dedupe only exact-name duplicates that commonly repeat; but since /mnt/data can't have same name,
# duplicates show up as different sources (e.g., multiple README.md but overwrote). Instead, we dedupe by content
# across files with same stem (README, package, tsconfig, admin_gamification, etc.) and keep one if identical.
# We'll create a content-addressable store and then create unique names.
content_map = {}  # hash -> kept relative path
manifest = []

for p in sorted(all_files, key=lambda x: x.name.lower()):
    # Skip staging artifacts if rerun
    if p.name.startswith("PURETASK_FINAL_BUNDLE_STAGING_"):
        continue
    if p == staging:
        continue
    cat = category_for(p)
    h = sha256_file(p)
    size = p.stat().st_size
    # Determine destination filename
    # If hash already seen, we won't copy again; but we'll note alias
    if h in content_map:
        # record as duplicate
        manifest.append({
            "source": p.name,
            "category": cat,
            "dest": content_map[h],
            "sha256": h,
            "size_bytes": size,
            "status": "duplicate_of_existing"
        })
        continue
    # unique content: copy into category folder
    dest_dir = staging / cat
    dest_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    dest_name = p.name
    dest_path = dest_dir / dest_name
    # Ensure no name collision inside category (unlikely). If collision, suffix with short hash.
    if dest_path.exists():
        dest_name = f"{p.stem}__{h[:8]}{p.suffix}"
        dest_path = dest_dir / dest_name
    shutil.copy2(p, dest_path)
    rel = str(dest_path.relative_to(staging))
    content_map[h] = rel
    manifest.append({
        "source": p.name,
        "category": cat,
        "dest": rel,
        "sha256": h,
        "size_bytes": size,
        "status": "copied"
    })

# Create manifest json + md summary
manifest_path = staging / "MANIFEST.json"
manifest_path.write_text(json.dumps({
    "generated_at_utc": datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
    "note": "This bundle contains only files present in the current environment (/mnt/data) at generation time.",
    "items": manifest
}, indent=2))

# Create migration run order doc
sql_files = [p for p in staging.glob("migrations/*.sql")]
def migration_sort_key(p: Path):
    n = p.name.lower()
    # extract step number or v number
    m = re.search(r"step\s*([0-9]+)", n) or re.search(r"step([0-9]+)", n) or re.search(r"v([0-9]+_[0-9]+)", n)
    if m:
        g = m.group(1)
        if g.isdigit():
            return (0, int(g), n)
        # v1_3 -> (1, 1.3)
        if "_" in g:
            a,b = g.split("_",1)
            try:
                return (1, float(f"{a}.{b}"), n)
            except:
                return (2, 0, n)
    # event tables first if present
    if "event_tables" in n:
        return (-1, 0, n)
    if "feature_flags" in n:
        return (3, 0, n)
    return (9, 0, n)

sql_files_sorted = sorted(sql_files, key=migration_sort_key)

migration_doc = staging / "docs" / "MIGRATION_RUN_ORDER.md"
migration_doc.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

lines = []
lines.append("# PureTask Gamification — Migration Run Order (Generated)\n")
lines.append(f"_Generated: {datetime.utcnow().isoformat()}Z_\n")
lines.append("This file lists SQL migrations included in this bundle, in a recommended safe order.\n")
lines.append("**Important:** Verify against your existing DB schema and migration framework before applying to production.\n\n")
lines.append("## Recommended order\n")
for i,p in enumerate(sql_files_sorted, start=1):
    rel = str(p.relative_to(staging))
    lines.append(f"{i}. `{rel}`\n")
lines.append("\n## Notes\n")
lines.append("- Run in staging first.\n")
lines.append("- If any migration already applied in your DB, ensure idempotency or skip accordingly.\n")
lines.append("- If you use a migrations table (e.g., knex/prisma/flyway), rename/move files into that framework.\n")
migration_doc.write_text("".join(lines))

# Create a PDF index
pdf_path = base / "PURETASK_FINAL_BUNDLE_INDEX.pdf"
styles = getSampleStyleSheet()
h1 = ParagraphStyle("h1", parent=styles["Heading1"], spaceAfter=10)
h2 = ParagraphStyle("h2", parent=styles["Heading2"], spaceAfter=8)
body = ParagraphStyle("body", parent=styles["BodyText"], spaceAfter=6, leading=12)

doc = SimpleDocTemplate(str(pdf_path), pagesize=LETTER, rightMargin=36, leftMargin=36, topMargin=36, bottomMargin=36)
story = []
story.append(Paragraph("PureTask Final Master Bundle — File Index", h1))
story.append(Paragraph(f"Generated (UTC): {datetime.utcnow().isoformat()}Z", body))
story.append(Spacer(1, 10))
story.append(Paragraph("This PDF is an index of the files included in the final bundle created from the current workspace.", body))
story.append(Paragraph("If some expected files are missing, they were not present in the workspace at generation time and must be re-uploaded.", body))
story.append(PageBreak())

# Group manifest by category
from collections import defaultdict
cat_map = defaultdict(list)
for item in manifest:
    if item["status"] == "copied":
        cat_map[item["category"]].append(item)

def human_size(n):
    for unit in ["B","KB","MB","GB"]:
        if n < 1024:
            return f"{n:.0f} {unit}"
        n /= 1024
    return f"{n:.1f} TB"

for cat in sorted(cat_map.keys()):
    story.append(Paragraph(cat.upper(), h2))
    items = cat_map[cat]
    # Table with first 40 chars of sha
    data = [["File (dest path)", "Size", "SHA256 (first 16)"]]
    for it in sorted(items, key=lambda x: x["dest"]):
        data.append([it["dest"], human_size(it["size_bytes"]), it["sha256"][:16]])
    tbl = Table(data, colWidths=[320, 80, 120])
    tbl.setStyle(TableStyle([
        ("BACKGROUND",(0,0),(-1,0), colors.lightgrey),
        ("GRID",(0,0),(-1,-1), 0.25, colors.grey),
        ("VALIGN",(0,0),(-1,-1), "TOP"),
        ("FONTSIZE",(0,0),(-1,-1), 8),
        ("FONTSIZE",(0,0),(-1,0), 9),
    ]))
    story.append(tbl)
    story.append(PageBreak())

doc.build(story)

# Create final archive zip with clean structure
final_zip = base / "PURETASK_FINAL_MASTER_BUNDLE.zip"
if final_zip.exists():
    final_zip.unlink()

with zipfile.ZipFile(final_zip, "w", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as z:
    for p in staging.rglob("*"):
        if p.is_file():
            z.write(p, arcname=str(p.relative_to(staging)))

# Also create a quick README inside docs (already have, but add bundle readme)
bundle_readme = staging / "docs" / "BUNDLE_README.md"
bundle_readme.write_text(
    "# PureTask Final Master Bundle\n\n"
    "This archive was generated from the current workspace files.\n\n"
    "## Folder structure\n"
    "- `docs/` : markdown specs, context, runbooks, wireframes\n"
    "- `configs/` : JSON configs and contracts\n"
    "- `migrations/` : SQL migrations\n"
    "- `code/` : TypeScript/JS snippets and services (loose files)\n"
    "- `step_zips/` : step implementation zip bundles\n"
    "- `pdfs/` : PDFs (workflow docs, conversations, canon PDFs)\n"
    "- `images/` : image assets\n"
    "- `data/` : CSV or similar\n"
    "- `misc/` : anything else\n\n"
    "## Integrity\n"
    "See `MANIFEST.json` for SHA256 hashes.\n"
)

# Update zip to include bundle readme + manifest changes
with zipfile.ZipFile(final_zip, "a", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as z:
    z.write(bundle_readme, arcname=str(bundle_readme.relative_to(staging)))
    z.write(manifest_path, arcname=str(manifest_path.relative_to(staging)))
    z.write(migration_doc, arcname=str(migration_doc.relative_to(staging)))

(str(final_zip), str(pdf_path), str(migration_doc), str(manifest_path))

👉 Almost everything you uploaded belongs in the BACKEND.

Let me break it down clearly.

✅ BACKEND (Server / API / Worker)
All of this goes in the backend repo:

1️⃣ Core Gamification Engine
goal_evaluator.ts

level_evaluator.ts

reward_granter.ts

reward_grant_service.ts

These are business logic.
They must live on the server.
Never in frontend.

2️⃣ Admin Control Plane
admin_config_service.ts

admin_budget_service.ts

admin_governor_service.ts

admin_feature_flag_service.ts

admin_audit_service.ts

rbac.ts

These are server-controlled controls.
Backend only.

3️⃣ API Routes
app.ts

cleaners_gamification.ts

admin_gamification.ts

client.ts

These define API endpoints.
Backend only.

4️⃣ Migrations (SQL)
All .sql files go in:

backend/
  migrations/
Never frontend.

5️⃣ Contracts & Configs
metrics_contract_v1.json

event_contract_v1.json

puretask_goal_definitions_v1_1.json

config_v1_2.json

etc.

These should live in backend config folder:

backend/
  config/
The frontend should NOT own these.

🟡 FRONTEND (Only these parts)
Frontend should only contain:

1️⃣ UI Rendering
Display:

Cleaner level

Progress bars

Goals

Badges

Active rewards

But it should NOT calculate them.

It should call backend endpoints like:

GET /cleaners/:id/progression
GET /cleaners/:id/badges
GET /cleaners/:id/rewards
2️⃣ Admin UI (Frontend)
If you build:

Admin dashboard

Governor tuning screen

Budget controls

The UI code goes frontend,
but the logic stays backend.

🔥 Extremely Important
Never put:

Goal evaluation

Reward granting

Acceptance rate math

Good-faith decline logic

Ranking multipliers

Governor calculations

in frontend.

That would allow:

cheating

tampering

trust issues

    |
    |  REST / GraphQL
    v
Backend (Node / TS)
    |
    |  SQL
    v
Database
Frontend = display layer
Backend = logic + rules
Database = state

# Deployment

**What it is:** How we deploy to Railway and manage production (environments, DB, CI/CD, rollback).  
**What it does:** Documents deploy flow so we can ship and roll back safely.  
**How we use it:** Follow when deploying; use rollback section when reverting.

---

## Exact backend structure for production

This is the **exact** process layout and commands for running the PureTask backend in production.

### Processes (what to run)

| Process | Command | Purpose |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **API server** | npm run build then npm start | Serves HTTP + Socket.IO. Entry: node -r ./dist/instrument.js ./dist/index.js. |
| **Scheduler** | npm run worker:scheduler | Either runs workers inline (cron) or **enqueues** to durable_jobs when CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY=true. |
| **Durable job worker** | npm run worker:durable-jobs:loop | **Required** if CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY=true. Consumes durable_jobs and runs enqueued workers. |

**Production recommendation:** Run **three** separate processes (or three Railway services):

1. **API** — npm run build && npm start (long-running).
2. **Scheduler** — npm run worker:scheduler (long-running; enqueues only when CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY=true).
3. **Worker** — npm run worker:durable-jobs:loop (long-running; processes the queue).

If CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY=false, you can run only **API** + **Scheduler** (scheduler runs workers directly on cron; no separate worker process).

### Build and start commands

bash
# One-time build (or in CI/Railway build step)
npm ci
npm run build

# Start API (production)
npm start
# → node -r ./dist/instrument.js ./dist/index.js

# Start scheduler (separate process)
npm run worker:scheduler
# → ts-node src/workers/scheduler.ts

# Start durable worker (separate process; required when CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY=true)
npm run worker:durable-jobs:loop
# → ts-node src/workers/runDurableJobWorker.ts --loop


### Required environment variables (production)

| Variable | Required | Notes |
|----------|----------|--------|
| NODE_ENV | Yes | production |
| PORT | Yes | e.g. 4000 (Railway sets automatically) |
| DATABASE_URL | Yes | Postgres connection string (Neon: use ?sslmode=require) |
| JWT_SECRET | Yes | Long random string (≥64 chars) |
| STRIPE_SECRET_KEY | Yes | Live key in prod |
| STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET | Yes | From Stripe webhook endpoint |
| N8N_WEBHOOK_SECRET | Yes | For n8n event verification |
| FRONTEND_URL | Yes | Production frontend origin (for CORS and links), e.g. https://app.puretask.com |
| APP_URL | Recommended | Same as frontend or API base URL for email links |
| SENTRY_DSN | Recommended | Error tracking |
| CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY | Recommended | true in prod so scheduler only enqueues; run worker separately |
| PAYOUTS_ENABLED | Optional | true when ready to process payouts (default false) |
| REDIS_URL | Optional | For rate limiting; if unset, in-memory limiter is used |

Optional: JWT_EXPIRES_IN, SENDGRID_*, TWILIO_*, WORKERS_ENABLED, BOOKINGS_ENABLED, etc. See src/config/env.ts and .env.example.

### Railway layout (example)

| Service | Build | Start | Env |
|---------|--------|--------|-----|
| **api** | npm ci && npm run build | npm start | All vars; PORT from Railway |
| **scheduler** | npm ci && npm run build | node -r ./dist/instrument.js ./dist/workers/scheduler.js | Same as api; no PORT needed |
| **worker** | npm ci && npm run build | node -r ./dist/instrument.js ./dist/workers/runDurableJobWorker.js --loop | Same as api; CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY=true |

If using **compiled JS** for scheduler/worker, point to dist/ (e.g. node dist/workers/scheduler.js). If using ts-node in prod, use npm run worker:scheduler and npm run worker:durable-jobs:loop (ensure ts-node is a dependency).

### Directory / artifact layout (after build)

puretask-backend/
├── dist/                    # Compiled output (npm run build)
│   ├── index.js             # API entry
│   ├── instrument.js        # Sentry (required by npm start)
│   ├── config/
│   ├── routes/
│   ├── services/
│   ├── workers/
│   │   ├── scheduler.js
│   │   └── runDurableJobWorker.js
│   └── ...
├── node_modules/
├── package.json
├── .env                     # Not in repo; set in Railway/host
└── DB/migrations/           # For migration runs


### Health and readiness

- **Liveness:** GET /health → 200, { ok: true }.
- **Readiness:** GET /health/ready → 200 when DB is reachable.
- Use these for Railway (or any platform) health checks and load balancer probes.

### One-line summary

**Production = API process + Scheduler process + Durable worker process** (with CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY=true), same env (including DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET, Stripe, FRONTEND_URL), build once with npm run build, start API with npm start, scheduler with worker:scheduler, worker with worker:durable-jobs:loop.

---

## Environments

- **local** — Development on your machine (see SETUP.md).
- **staging** (if used) — Pre-production; same stack as prod, different env vars.
- **production** — Live API used by users.

## Railway setup

1. **Create project** — Railway Dashboard → Deploy from GitHub repo → select puretask-backend.
2. **Database** — Add Neon (or Railway Postgres); DATABASE_URL is set automatically or add it in Variables.
3. **Variables** — In the service → Variables tab, set at least:
   - DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET, STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET, N8N_WEBHOOK_SECRET
   - Notifications: SENDGRID_API_KEY, SENDGRID_FROM_EMAIL, TWILIO_*, N8N_WEBHOOK_URL (as needed)
   - NODE_ENV=production
   - **SENTRY_DSN** — For error tracking in production (Sentry Dashboard → Project Settings → DSN)
4. **Build/start** — Usually: build npm run build, start node -r ./dist/instrument.js ./dist/index.js (see package.json scripts).

Railway auto-deploys on push to the linked branch unless disabled.

## Database

- **Neon** — Use the connection string from Neon dashboard; ensure SSL (?sslmode=require in DATABASE_URL).
- **Migrations in prod** — Run migrations as part of deploy or via a one-off job; avoid breaking schema changes in a single deploy.
- **Schema verify** — After patch or restore: PRODUCTION_DATABASE_URL=... npm run db:verify:production.
- **Backup/restore** — See [BACKUP_RESTORE.md](./BACKUP_RESTORE.md). Neon PITR; restore drill: create branch from restore point → update DATABASE_URL → run schema verify → smoke test.

## CI/CD

- **GitHub Actions** — Lint, test, build on push/PR (.github/workflows/ci.yml, .github/workflows/test.yml).
- **Migrations check** — .github/workflows/migrations.yml applies consolidated schema on fresh Postgres; runs on push/PR to main/develop.
- **Deploy trigger** — Railway deploys on push to main (or configured branch).
- **Staging (optional)** — Create a second Railway service or project with DATABASE_URL pointing to a staging Neon branch. Deploy develop → staging; merge main → production.
- **Coverage** — CI runs npm run test:coverage; reports in coverage/. Add CODECOV_TOKEN secret and lcov.info will be uploaded. Badge: [![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/OWNER/REPO/graph/badge.svg)](https://codecov.io/gh/OWNER/REPO).

### Branch protection (recommended)

In GitHub → **Settings** → **Branches** → **Add rule** for main:

1. **Require a pull request** before merging (at least 1 approval).
2. **Require status checks** to pass: lint, test, build.
3. **Do not allow bypassing** (optional, for stricter policy).
4. **Include administrators** (optional).

This prevents direct pushes and ensures CI runs before merge.

**Verification checklist:**
- [ ] Branch protection rule exists for main
- [ ] Required status checks: lint, test, build (or your workflow job names)
- [ ] At least 1 approval required for PRs

---

## Stripe webhook (production)

1. **Stripe Dashboard** → Developers → Webhooks → Add endpoint.
2. **URL:** https://<your-api-domain>/api/webhooks/stripe/webhook (e.g. https://api.puretask.com/api/webhooks/stripe/webhook).
3. **Events:** Select events you handle (e.g. payment_intent.succeeded, charge.refunded, account.updated).
4. **Signing secret:** Copy to Railway STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET.
5. **Verify:** Stripe sends a test event; backend returns 200. Resend an event from Dashboard to confirm idempotency (no double-processing).

**Verification checklist:**
- [ ] Webhook endpoint URL matches production API domain
- [ ] STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET set in Railway (from Stripe webhook signing secret)
- [ ] Test event returns 200 in Stripe Dashboard
- [ ] Events list includes all required: payment_intent.succeeded, charge.refunded, account.updated (and others per your handlers)

---

## Workers and CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY

If CRONS_ENQUEUE_ONLY=true (scheduler enqueues only):

- The **scheduler** (worker:scheduler) enqueues jobs into durable_jobs.
- The **durable job worker** must run separately: worker:durable-jobs (one cycle) or worker:durable-jobs:loop (continuous).
- On Railway: add a second service or cron job that runs npm run worker:durable-jobs:loop (or a periodic worker:durable-jobs).
- Without the worker, enqueued jobs never execute.

## Rollback

When a deploy causes errors:

1. **Railway** → Project → Service → **Deployments**.
2. Find the **last known good** deployment → **Redeploy** (or Rollback).
3. Verify: GET /health and GET /health/ready → 200.
4. Do **not** run “migrate down” unless you have a tested, safe down migration.

Full steps: [docs/runbooks/rollback-deploy.md](../runbooks/rollback-deploy.md).

### Migration safety

- Run migrations check locally: DATABASE_URL=... npm run db:setup:test then npm run test.
- CI migrations.yml applies 000_CONSOLIDATED_SCHEMA.sql on fresh DB; passes if tables exist.
- For additive migrations: apply forward; older code can run with new schema.
- For breaking changes: deploy in two phases (add column → deploy code that uses it → remove old column).

## Production verification checklist

Before going live or after deploy, verify:

| Item | How to verify |
|------|---------------|
| **Stripe webhook** | Stripe Dashboard → Webhooks → Send test event; confirm 200 response |
| **SENTRY_DSN** | Sentry project receives errors; trigger test error or wait for real one |
| **Branch protection** | GitHub → Settings → Branches; confirm rule exists and status checks required |
| **Health** | GET /health and GET /health/ready return 200 |
| **DB connectivity** | Health ready check includes DB ping |

## Launch readiness (Section 14)

- **Feature flags:** admin_feature_flags (gamification), env kill switches (BOOKINGS_ENABLED, PAYOUTS_ENABLED, etc.). See RUNBOOK § 3.2.
- **Rollout plan:** Staging → internal dogfood → city pilot → scale. See RUNBOOK § 5.
- **Incident runbook:** RUNBOOK § 3.1; SECURITY_INCIDENT_RESPONSE.md for secrets exposure.

---

## Pre-launch preparation (step-by-step)

**Time:** ~30 min local prep, ~2.5 h for first full deploy. Use this before first production deploy or when bringing a new environment up.

### 1. Apply database indexes (~5 min)

- Run: psql $DATABASE_URL -f DB/migrations/030_performance_indexes.sql (or run the file in Neon SQL Editor).
- Expect: multiple CREATE INDEX; NOTICE about 35+ indexes.

### 2. Restart backend (~1 min)

- Stop: Ctrl+C in the terminal running the backend.
- Start: npm run dev (local) or use your normal start command so new routes/config are loaded.

### 3. Test API (~2 min)

- Health: GET /health and GET /health/ready return 200.
- From frontend repo (if available): run API smoke tests (e.g. npx ts-node tests/api/quick-api-test.ts or equivalent) and confirm search/auth/jobs as needed.

### 4. Manual UI smoke test (~10 min)

- Open frontend (e.g. http://localhost:3001).
- Test: login, search/browse, navigation, mobile view (F12 → device toolbar). Check console for errors.

### 5. Production config (env vars)

- **Backend (e.g. Railway):** NODE_ENV=production, DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET (new, long), Stripe **live** keys and STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET, FRONTEND_URL / APP_URL, optional: REDIS_URL, SENDGRID_*, SENTRY_DSN.
- **Frontend (e.g. Vercel):** NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL (production API URL), NEXT_PUBLIC_STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY (live), NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_ENVIRONMENT=production.

### 6. Deploy order

1. Push backend → deploy backend (e.g. Railway). Verify: curl https://<api-url>/health → 200.
2. Set frontend NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL to that API URL → push frontend → deploy (e.g. Vercel).
3. (Optional) Custom domain: add domain in Vercel/Railway, add DNS records at registrar, then update CORS origins in backend to include production domain and redeploy.

### 7. Post-deploy checks

- Health and ready: 200.
- Frontend: load site, login, search, key flows; confirm no CORS/404/500 in network tab.
- Stripe webhook: send test event from Stripe Dashboard; backend returns 200.

### Hosting reference

- **Backend:** Railway (Hobby/Pro). Deploy from GitHub; set env vars; use health URL for frontend.
- **Frontend:** Vercel (Free/Pro). Import repo; set NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL and other public env; deploy.
- **Domain:** Optional; add in Vercel/Railway and point DNS.

Detailed troubleshooting (DB index failures, deploy failures, CORS, 404s): see TROUBLESHOOTING.md and Rollback above.

wats is a runbook?

Great question.

A runbook is simply:

📘 A step-by-step instruction manual for handling a specific operational situation.

Think of it like a playbook for engineers.

In simple terms
If something breaks at 2am…

The runbook tells you:

What happened

How to diagnose it

Exactly what commands to run

How to fix it safely

How to roll back if needed

No guessing.
No improvising.
No panic.

Real-world analogy
If PureTask were an airline:

The product specs = how the plane is designed

The code = the plane

The runbook = the emergency procedures manual

When something goes wrong, you don’t “figure it out.”
You follow the procedure.

Examples in your PureTask system
You already referenced runbooks in your deployment doc.

Here’s what they would cover:

1️⃣ Rollback Runbook
If a deployment breaks production:

Go to Railway → Deployments

Redeploy last known good version

Check /health

Confirm Stripe webhook working

Confirm DB connectivity

That’s a runbook.

2️⃣ Stripe Webhook Failure Runbook
If Stripe payments stop working:

Check Railway logs

Verify STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET

Re-send test event from Stripe dashboard

Confirm 200 response

Check idempotency behavior

That’s a runbook.

3️⃣ Database Migration Failure Runbook
If a migration fails:

Stop deploy

Restore from Neon backup branch

Verify schema

Reapply safe migration

Smoke test critical endpoints

That’s a runbook.

Why runbooks matter (especially for you)
You’re building:

Background workers

Scheduled jobs

Durable queues

Stripe webhooks

Marketplace Health Governor

Feature flags

Reward engines

That is a serious production system.

Without runbooks:

Debugging becomes chaos

Every incident feels new

Scaling becomes dangerous

With runbooks:

You scale calmly

Anyone on your team can respond

You protect revenue and trust

How runbooks are usually organized
In your backend repo:

docs/
  DEPLOYMENT.md
  RUNBOOKS/
    rollback-deploy.md
    stripe-webhook.md
    db-migration.md
    governor-failure.md
    payout-freeze.md
Each one answers:

When do I use this?

What are the symptoms?

What exact commands do I run?

How do I confirm it worked?

The important mindset shift
Specs explain how something should work.

Runbooks explain what to do when it doesn’t.

That’s the difference.

If you want, I can now create:

A full PureTask Production Runbook Pack

Tailored specifically to your scheduler, workers, Stripe, payouts, and governor

That would make your backend enterprise-grade.

## 1. What you uploaded (the bundle)

You uploaded the **PureTask Gamification Master Bundle** (from Desktop\New folder). It contained:

### 1.2 What the bundle was designed to do

- Define the **event stream** (pt_event_log, pt_engagement_sessions, pt_engagement_actions, pt_safety_reports) for metrics.
- Define **goals, levels, rewards** via JSON config and **evaluators** (goal_evaluator, level_evaluator, reward_granter).

- **Runtime:** load active config from DB; poll; rollback = new version.

The bundle assumed **UUID** for user IDs and a separate **admin_users** table. It did not assume an existing users table with TEXT id or existing job_offers / safety_reports schema.

## 2. What already existed in this backend (before the merge)

### 2.1 Database (migrations 043–056 and consolidated schema)

- **043:** Cleaner level system (cleaner_level_definitions, cleaner_level_goals, cleaner_level_progress, cleaner_goal_completions, cleaner_rewards_granted, cleaner_login_days, cleaner_active_boosts, etc.).
- **046:** safety_reports (cleaner_id TEXT → users(id), job_offer_id, note, photo_url, created_at).
- **047:** pt_event_log, pt_engagement_sessions, pt_engagement_actions — **TEXT** cleaner_id/client_id and FKs to **users(id)**. No pt_safety_reports.
- **048:** cleaner_goal_progress, gamification_reward_grants, gamification_choice_eligibilities, view gamification_active_rewards — **TEXT** cleaner_id → users(id).
- **049:** SQL helpers (pt_haversine_meters, pt_haversine_miles, pt_within_radius_meters).
- **050:** Idempotency indexes, choice expires_at, views gamification_active_reward_grants / gamification_cleaner_active_rewards, function gamification_use_reward.

- **052–056:** Cash budget enforcement, badges, seasonal challenges (season_rules + season_application_log), ops views, marketplace health governor.

So the backend **already had** the same concepts (events, engagement, rewards, choices, seasons, admin, governor) but with **TEXT user IDs**, **users(id)** for admin, **safety_reports** (not pt_safety_reports), and **season_rules** (not gamification_season_rules).

- No event contract, metrics contract, or spec-enforcement matrix docs before the merge.

## 3. What we did (merge as reference — we did NOT overwrite)

We did **not** overwrite your existing data or replace your running pipeline. We:

1. **Copied bundle docs** → docs/active/gamification_bundle/ and docs/active/gamification_bundle/docs/ (all 12 files).
2. **Copied bundle SQL** → DB/migrations/bundle_reference/ (reference only; **did not run** these migrations).
3. **Copied bundle configs** (contracts only) → src/config/cleanerLevels/contracts/ (event_contract_v1.json, metrics_contract_v1.json, event_to_metric_mapping_v1.json). We did **not** overwrite goals.json, levels.json, or rewards.json.
4. **Copied bundle code** → src/gamification-bundle/ (reference only). We did **not** replace src/lib/gamification/, src/services/, or src/routes/gamification.ts.
5. **Merged canonical rules into docs** — We took the bundle’s **rules and constants** (meaningful login 15 min, message 25 chars, on-time ±15 min, GPS 250 m, good-faith 6/7 days, etc.) and added them to ARCHITECTURE §3.5 and RUNBOOK §4. So the *content* of the bundle docs that was “more complete” is now in your canonical docs; the bundle docs themselves sit alongside as reference.
6. **Documented the migration diff** — DB/migrations/bundle_reference/README.md explains exactly what differs between bundle SQL and 043–056 (pt_safety_reports, UUID vs TEXT, admin_users vs users, etc.).

So: **nothing that runs in production was overwritten.** The app still uses the same DB schema (043–056), the same config (cleanerLevels/*.json), and the same code (lib/gamification, services, routes, workers). The bundle exists as **reference and extra docs**.

So: **no production code was overwritten.** The bundle code is in **src/gamification-bundle/** and is **not** imported by the main app. The pipeline that runs is still the original backend (lib/gamification, services, routes, workers).

---

## 5. How the backend gamification works today (what actually runs)

1. **Config:** Loaded from **src/config/cleanerLevels/** (goals.json, levels.json, rewards.json, etc.) and optionally from DB via **runtimeConfigLoader** (admin_config_versions).
2. **Events:** App and workers write to **pt_event_log**, **pt_engagement_sessions**, **pt_engagement_actions** (047). Good-faith safety evidence goes to **safety_reports** (046).
3. **Metrics:** **metricsCalculator** / **gamificationMetrics** compute metric values from DB (jobs, clock-in/out, photos, messages, etc.).
4. **Goals / levels:** **cleanerGoalsService**, **gamificationProgressionService**, and **lib/gamification** (goal_evaluator, level_evaluator) evaluate progress and level eligibility.
5. **Rewards:** **gamificationRewardService**, **reward_granter**, **rewardEffectsService** grant and apply rewards (visibility, early exposure, fee discounts, etc.); **cashBudgetService** enforces caps; **feePolicyService** applies fee/instant-payout effects.
6. **Workers:** **processCleanerGamification** (and scheduler) expire rewards/choices, persist progress, grant rewards idempotently; **goalChecker** runs on job completion / triggers.
7. **Admin:** **adminConfigService**, **adminBudgetService**, **adminAuditService**, **admin_feature_flags**, **region_governor_config** — all use **users(id)**; no admin_users table.
8. **APIs:** Routes under **gamification.ts**, **admin/gamificationControl.ts**, **governor.ts**, **cleanerEnhanced.ts** expose progression, rewards, effects, debug, and admin controls.

All of that is **unchanged** by the merge. The bundle did not replace any of it.

---

## 6. What “overwrite everything with the new data” would mean

If you **did** overwrite everything with the bundle:

- **Docs:** You’d replace or heavily rewrite ARCHITECTURE, RUNBOOK, SETUP with the bundle’s docs. You’d lose backend-specific wiring (paths, service names, Trust API, etc.) unless you merged by hand.
- **Migrations:** You **must not** run the bundle SQL as-is (UUID vs TEXT, admin_users, pt_safety_reports, different season table). So “overwrite” for DB would mean either ignoring bundle SQL and keeping 043–056, or writing new migrations that match the bundle’s *intent* but with TEXT and users(id) — which is what 043–056 already do.
- **Config:** You’d overwrite goals.json, levels.json, rewards.json with the bundle’s versions. That could change goal IDs, reward IDs, and level rules and break existing progress or rewards if they’re tied to those IDs.
- **Code:** You’d replace src/lib/gamification, parts of services, and maybe routes with the bundle’s code. The bundle code expects UUID and admin_users and different imports; you’d have to refactor it to use TEXT and users(id) and your existing DB/client. So “overwrite” would be a large, error-prone refactor, not a drop-in replace.

So “overwrite everything” is **not** a safe one-step operation. The backend is already aligned with the bundle’s *behavior* and *schema intent* (with better fit to your DB and auth). What we did instead was **merge the bundle as reference** and **pull only the better doc content** (rules and constants) into your canonical docs.

---

## 7. What you should do before any “switch” or overwrite

### 7.1 Create a GitHub backup (recommended)

So you can always revert to “before the switch”:

1. **Commit any uncommitted changes** (so the backup is clean):
   - git status
   - git add . and git commit -m "Pre-bundle-switch state" if needed.

2. **Create a backup branch** (e.g. keeps current main as-is; backup is a named branch):
   - git checkout -b backup-before-gamification-bundle-switch
   - git push -u origin backup-before-gamification-bundle-switch

3. **Tag the same commit** (optional, for a clear “restore point”):
   - git tag pre-gamification-bundle-switch
   - git push origin pre-gamification-bundle-switch

4. **Go back to main** (or your main branch) to keep working:
   - git checkout main

If you ever need to “undo” a switch:  
git checkout backup-before-gamification-bundle-switch  
or  
git checkout pre-gamification-bundle-switch

### 7.2 Do you need to “overwrite” at all?

- **Current state:** Backend has the full pipeline (events, metrics, goals, levels, rewards, admin, workers). Bundle content is merged as **reference** (docs, contract JSONs, bundle_reference migrations, src/gamification-bundle code). Canonical rules and constants from the bundle are in ARCHITECTURE §3.5 and RUNBOOK §4.
- **Recommendation:** **Do not overwrite** production code or config with the bundle. Use the bundle as reference and, if needed, copy specific pieces (e.g. a new metric or goal shape) into your existing codebase after testing. If you still want a “bundle-first” codebase, do it on a **new branch** after creating the backup above, and plan for refactoring bundle code to use TEXT and users(id) and your existing services.

## 8. Short reference: where everything lives now

| What | Location |
|------|----------|
| **Bundle docs** (reference) | docs/active/gamification_bundle/ and docs/active/gamification_bundle/docs/ |
| **Bundle SQL** (reference only; do not run) | DB/migrations/bundle_reference/ |
| **Bundle code** (reference only; not wired) | src/gamification-bundle/ |
| **Contract JSONs** (from bundle) | src/config/cleanerLevels/contracts/ |
| **Canonical rules + constants** (from bundle, now canonical) | ARCHITECTURE §3.5, RUNBOOK §4 |
| **Migration diff (bundle vs 043–056)** | DB/migrations/bundle_reference/README.md |
| **What actually runs** | Same as before: 043–056 schema, src/config/cleanerLevels/*.json, src/lib/gamification, src/services, src/routes, src/workers |

---

## 9. Backup confirmed + When to overwrite with the bundle

### 9.1 You have a version you can restore

After running the backup steps and **pushing main**:

- **main** on GitHub = current state (bundle merged as reference, all your work).
- **backup-before-gamification-bundle-switch** = same commit as that main; a named branch you can always checkout.
- **pre-gamification-bundle-switch** (tag) = same commit; a fixed restore point.

If something goes wrong later, restore with either:

- git checkout backup-before-gamification-bundle-switch  
- or git checkout pre-gamification-bundle-switch

So you do have a version you can upload/restore from if needed.

### 9.2 Why overwriting with the bundle can be the right choice

You and an AI designed the bundle around how you want PureTask to actually run: rules, events, metrics, goals, rewards, admin, and pipeline. If that design is your source of truth, **overwriting the backend to match the bundle is a valid and good goal.** The earlier “don’t overwrite” note was about **how** to do it (safely), not whether the bundle should win. If you want the bundle’s behavior and docs to be canonical, the backend should be brought in line with it in a planned way.

### 9.3 Why it isn’t a “one-line” switch — and what the steps are

It’s not one line because:

1. **DB:** The bundle SQL uses UUID and admin_users; your live DB uses **TEXT** and **users(id)**. Running the bundle SQL as-is would conflict with existing schema. So the “overwrite” for DB is: **keep 043–056** (they already implement the bundle’s intent with your schema) and only add or change what’s missing (e.g. we decided not to add pt_safety_reports; you could add it in one migration if you want).
2. **Config:** Replacing goals.json/rewards.json/levels.json with the bundle’s versions in one go could change goal/reward IDs and break existing progress or rewards. So the overwrite needs a **config migration plan** (e.g. map old IDs to new, or deploy in a backward-compatible way).
3. **Code:** The bundle code expects UUID and different imports/DB shape. To “overwrite” with it you have to **adapt** it (TEXT, users(id), your routes/DB client) and then **wire** it in place of (or merged with) the current services. That’s many file edits and tests, not one command.

So the “lines” are **steps**, not shell commands. A safe overwrite looks like this:

---

### 9.4 Step-by-step plan to overwrite with the bundle (the “lines”)

Do this on a **new branch** (e.g. bundle-as-canonical) so main stays restorable. Each step is one logical “line” of work (possibly many file edits).

1. **Branch and backup**  
   Create bundle-as-canonical from main. Backup already exists (branch + tag); push main so GitHub has it.

2. **Docs: make bundle docs canonical**  
   - Replace or rewrite ARCHITECTURE gamification sections so they match the bundle’s Cursor context and flow.  
   - Replace or merge RUNBOOK gamification sections with the bundle’s runbook/ops content.  
   - Use bundle’s event_contract and metrics_contract as the canonical spec; keep or merge SETUP/DEPLOYMENT so they still match this repo.

3. **Config: adopt bundle goals/rewards/levels**  
   - Diff bundle’s puretask_goal_definitions_v1_1.json and config_v1_2 (or config.json) with current goals.json, levels.json, rewards.json.  
   - Decide: full replace (and plan for existing progress/rewards if IDs change) or merge bundle additions into existing IDs.  
   - Overwrite or merge the JSON files; update any code that assumes old goal/reward IDs.

4. **DB: no bundle SQL as-is**  
   - Do **not** run bundle migrations directly.  
   - Keep 043–056. If you need something from the bundle that’s missing (e.g. pt_safety_reports), add one new migration (e.g. 057) that matches your schema (TEXT, users(id)).

5. **Code: adapt and wire bundle code**  
   - In src/gamification-bundle/, change every cleaner_id/client_id type and usage from UUID to **TEXT** and ensure FKs/queries use **users(id)**.  
   - Replace or merge src/lib/gamification/ and the relevant parts of src/services/ (e.g. cleanerGoalsService, gamificationRewardService, rewardEffectsService) with the adapted bundle code.  
   - Keep your Express routes and DB client; have them call the new/bundle-based services.  
   - Remove or refactor any dependency on admin_users so admin tables keep using **users(id)**.

6. **Workers and pipeline**  
   - Point the gamification worker (and any cron) at the new services and config.  
   - Ensure event ingestion (pt_event_log, pt_engagement_*) and metrics use the bundle’s event/metric contracts.

7. **Tests and QA**  
   - Run unit and integration tests.  
   - Manually test: progression, reward grants, admin config, governor, debug endpoints.

8. **Deploy**  
   - Deploy the new branch to staging first; then to production when satisfied.  
   - If you need to roll back, checkout backup-before-gamification-bundle-switch or tag pre-gamification-bundle-switch and redeploy that.

So: **8 steps** (8 “lines”), each step possibly many edits. The important part is: backup first (done), then do the overwrite on a branch in this order so DB stays consistent, config is migrated, and code is adapted to your schema before you rely on it.

wat is our src/lib/gamification

Great question.

src/lib/gamification is the core brain of your progression engine.
If your backend were a body:

routes/ = the mouth (APIs)

services/ = the muscles (business logic wiring)

workers/ = the heartbeat (background processing)

src/lib/gamification/ = the brain

It contains the pure logic engine that decides:

Is this goal complete?

Is this level unlocked?

Which rewards should be granted?

Is it idempotent?

What metrics count?

What lives inside src/lib/gamification
Based on your backend structure, this folder typically contains:

1️⃣ goal_evaluator.ts
What it does:
Takes:

metric values (e.g., jobs_completed = 12)

goal definition (e.g., >= 10 jobs)

Returns:

complete / incomplete

progress percentage

This is where:

meaningful login

25-char message rule

1 before + 1 after photo rule

add-on counts

good-faith decline exclusions

are evaluated.

2️⃣ level_evaluator.ts
What it does:
Checks:

Are all Core goals complete?

Is at least one Stretch goal complete?

Are Maintenance rules satisfied?

If yes → level eligible.

This is what enforces:

“levels never go down”

maintenance pauses progression but does not remove level

3️⃣ reward_granter.ts
What it does:

After a level or goal is achieved:

Determines which rewards to grant

Ensures idempotency (no double grants)

Creates reward grant records

Applies expiration (temporary boosts)

Handles choice rewards

4️⃣ metricProviderAdapter.ts
What it does:

Acts as the bridge between:

Raw DB metrics

Goal evaluator

It translates:
“Count jobs where on_time = true”
into:
metrics.on_time_jobs = 14

5️⃣ types.ts
Defines the canonical shapes:

GoalDefinition

LevelDefinition

RewardDefinition

MetricValue

EvaluationResult

This keeps everything strongly typed.

How it fits into your real pipeline
Here’s the real production flow:

Cleaner completes a job

Event written to pt_event_log

Worker (processCleanerGamification)

Calls:

metric calculator

goal_evaluator

level_evaluator

reward_granter

Writes:

progress

reward grants

active effects

Why this folder is important
This is the pure engine layer.

It:

does not know about Express

does not know about HTTP

does not know about Railway

does not know about Stripe

It only knows:

“Given metrics and config, what should happen?”

That’s good architecture.

Difference from src/gamification-bundle/
You also have:

src/gamification-bundle/
That is:

a reference implementation copy

not wired into production

src/lib/gamification/ is the one actually running.

Extremely important
If you ever “overwrite with bundle code”:

This is the folder that would be replaced or refactored.

Not routes.
Not migrations.
Not workers.

This is the engine core.

If you want, I can now:

Draw a full visual architecture diagram of how lib/gamification connects to workers, services, and DB

Or audit whether your current engine matches the bundle rules exactly

Or explain how to refactor this safely without breaking rewards

Just tell me which direction you want to go.
